This perspective shows that, although OPD provides effective token-level correction, its ceiling is constrained by the absence of trajectory-level discrimination. Motivated by these observations, we propose V-Zero, an answer-label-free framework for visual reasoning with contrastive evidence gating. V-Zero uses no annotated textual answer labels; instead, during training it pairs a question-relevant regional crop with a negative visual view to evaluate student-sampled trajectories and gate dense token-level distillation. Experiments on multiple visual reasoning benchmarks show that V-Zero consistently improves fine-grained visual reasoning while preserving strong generalization. Notably, V-Zero is more than 5$\times$ faster than previous supervised fine-tuning methods and more than 10$\times$ faster than reinforcement learning baselines. Code and dataset will be released at https://github.com/eVI-group-SCU/V-Zero

AI.tldr: V-Zero is an answer-label-free framework for fine-grained visual reasoning that uses contrastive evidence gating with on-policy distillation, achieving 5-10x faster training than supervised or RL methods while maintaining strong generalization.
AI.motivation: Existing agentic visual reasoning methods rely on costly reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards or supervised fine-tuning on large-scale annotated reasoning traces, which require expensive exploration, hand-designed rules, or heavy textual supervision. The authors aim to avoid external answer labels entirely.
AI.method: V-Zero revisits On-Policy Distillation (OPD) as negative-free stop-gradient alignment, revealing its ceiling due to lack of trajectory-level discrimination. It pairs a question-relevant regional crop with a negative visual view during training to evaluate student-sampled trajectories and gate dense token-level distillation without annotated answer labels.
AI.result: Consistent improvement on fine-grained visual reasoning benchmarks with strong generalization. More than 5x faster than supervised fine-tuning and more than 10x faster than reinforcement learning baselines.
AI.conclusion: V-Zero demonstrates that effective visual reasoning can be achieved without any annotated answer labels by combining on-policy distillation with contrastive evidence gating, offering a practical and efficient alternative to existing approaches.

title: Efficient Remote Sensing Instance Segmentation with Linear-Time State Space Distilled Visual Foundation Models
comment: 17 pages, 11 figures, has been published in IEEE TGRS vol. 64, pp. 5625417-5625417, 2026, Art no. 5625417, doi: 10.1109/TGRS.2026.3696104
summary: The computational complexity of Transformers scales quadratically with the number of tokens, which significantly constrains the efficiency of vision models, particularly recent ViT-based foundation models in dense prediction tasks. Instance segmentation, a typical dense visual prediction task in the remote sensing field, faces similar challenges. In this paper, inspired by the recent advances of knowledge distillation in large language models, we introduce RS4D - a new remote sensing instance segmentation method with linear computational complexity, which addresses the inefficiency of long sequence modeling through distilled state space modeling (SSM). We propose an adaptive noise and masking knowledge distillation training method for pre-training lightweight SSM backbones, which effectively compresses knowledge from the vast self-attention space into a compact, dense linear state space. We also design a remote sensing image instance segmentation architecture based on this lightweight visual encoder, where we explore variants of three different backbones and two segmentation heads. Extensive experiments are conducted on multiple benchmark datasets, including SSDD, WHU, and NWPU. Compared to ViT-based approaches, our proposed SSM backbone achieves an 8x reduction in parameters and a 9x reduction in FLOPs while maintaining comparable or superior accuracy to both ViT- and CNN-based instance segmentation methods. The implementation codes have been publicly available at https://github.com/QinzheYang/RS4D.

AI.tldr: RS4D is a remote sensing instance segmentation method using distilled state space modeling (SSM) that achieves linear computational complexity, reducing parameters by 8x and FLOPs by 9x compared to ViT-based approaches while maintaining comparable accuracy on SSDD, WHU, and NWPU datasets.
AI.motivation: Transformers' quadratic complexity with token length limits efficiency of ViT-based vision models, especially for dense prediction tasks like instance segmentation in remote sensing.
AI.method: Uses distilled state space modeling (SSM) with an adaptive noise and masking knowledge distillation training method to compress self-attention knowledge into a linear state space, combined with a lightweight visual encoder for instance segmentation.
AI.result: 8x parameter reduction and 9x FLOPs reduction compared to ViT methods, with comparable or superior accuracy to both ViT- and CNN-based instance segmentation methods on SSDD, WHU, and NWPU benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: State space modeling with knowledge distillation offers an efficient linear-complexity alternative to quadratic-complexity Transformers for remote sensing instance segmentation.

title: State Space Models Meet Remote Sensing: A Survey
comment: 25 pages, 5 figures, has been published in SCIS SCIQ1 IF=8.1 https://doi.org/10.1007/s11432-025-4780-1
summary: State Space Models (SSMs), designed for long-range modeling, offer linear computational complexity and strong capabilities in capturing long-range dependencies. In the field of remote sensing, SSMs have gained popularity due to their effectiveness in addressing unique challenges such as dense visual predictions, multi-modal remote sensing data, and temporal remote sensing data, which have also yielded significant advancements in customized architectures. This paper presents a comprehensive review of SSM-based approaches in remote sensing, covering most of the relevant studies since SSMs were first introduced to the field. We offer a multi-dimensional analysis examining SSM applications in remote sensing tasks and discussing advancements in architecture design. This paper not only synthesizes the rapid progress in SSM-based research but also identifies key challenges and future opportunities. By providing a detailed perspective, this paper aims to serve as a foundational resource for remote sensing researchers, offering actionable insights to foster further advancements in this evolving domain. We will keep tracing related works at https://github.com/QinzheYang/Awesome-RS-State-Space-Model.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive survey of State Space Models (SSMs) applied to remote sensing, covering applications, architecture designs, challenges, and future opportunities.
AI.motivation: SSMs have shown promise for long-range modeling in remote sensing tasks (dense predictions, multi-modal, temporal data), but the field lacks a systematic review to synthesize rapid progress and guide future research.
AI.method: Multi-dimensional analysis reviewing SSM-based architectures for remote sensing tasks since their introduction, categorizing applications and architectural advancements.
AI.result: Identifies key SSM successes across remote sensing tasks, summarizes architectural innovations, and outlines remaining challenges and research opportunities.
AI.conclusion: This review serves as a foundational resource for remote sensing researchers, providing actionable insights to foster further SSM advancements; the authors maintain a public repository to track ongoing work.

title: Invoice Haystack: Benchmarking Document Retrieval and Visual Question Answering Under Strong Visual Homogeneity
summary: Vision Language Models have achieved near-human performance on single-document Visual Question Answering, yet their effectiveness degrades significantly when retrieving information from large collections of visually homogeneous documents. Existing multi-document benchmarks aggregate diverse document types, creating artificial separation in embedding space that does not reflect enterprise document repositories where thousands of records share identical visual templates. We identify this as embedding collapse and introduce Invoice Haystack, a benchmark with 1,500 anonymized invoice images paired with 200 discriminative question-answer pairs, specifically designed to stress-test retrieval under strong visual homogeneity. Invoice Haystack exhibits a mean pairwise cosine similarity of 0.73, compared to 0.38 (DocHaystack) and 0.31 (InfoHaystack) in existing benchmarks, posing a fundamentally more challenging retrieval problem. Addressing the identified challenge, we propose VL-RAG, a hybrid retrieval-augmented generation framework that jointly leverages text and visual embeddings to harness the complementary strengths of both modalities, followed by a VLM-based verification filter for precise document identification. VL-RAG achieves 60.0\% Recall@1 on Invoice Haystack-500, outperforming existing state-of-the-art method by up to an absolute 13.5 percentage points. It further improves retrieval considerably on DocHaystack-1000 (77.1\% vs.\ 75.2\%) and InfoHaystack-1000 (84.5\% vs.\ 80.0\%), establishing the proposed dual-stream fusion as a consistently superior retrieval strategy across both homogeneous and heterogeneous document collections.

AI.tldr: VL-RAG, a dual-stream retrieval framework combining text and visual embeddings with VLM verification, significantly improves multi-document VQA retrieval on visually homogeneous documents (e.g., invoices), achieving 60% Recall@1 on the new Invoice Haystack benchmark (+13.5 points over SOTA).
AI.motivation: Existing VLMs struggle to retrieve information from large collections of visually homogeneous documents (e.g., enterprise invoices) because current benchmarks artificially separate documents by type, causing an "embedding collapse" where visually similar documents become indistinguishable in embedding space.
AI.method: (1) Invoice Haystack benchmark: 1,500 anonymized invoices with 200 QA pairs, mean pairwise cosine similarity 0.73. (2) VL-RAG: hybrid RAG framework fusing text and visual embeddings for retrieval, plus a VLM-based verification filter for precise document identification.
AI.result: VL-RAG achieves 60.0% Recall@1 on Invoice Haystack-500 (+13.5 points), and improves DocHaystack-1000 (77.1% vs 75.2%) and InfoHaystack-1000 (84.5% vs 80.0%).
AI.conclusion: Dual-stream fusion of text and visual embeddings is a consistently superior retrieval strategy for both homogeneous and heterogeneous document collections.

title: Follow Your Track: Precise Skeleton Animation Controlled by 3D Trajectories
summary: 4D generation aims to animate 3D objects with realistic motion, holding great promise for applications. Existing methods typically decouple 3D asset generation from motion synthesis: acquire a 3D asset, prepare a structural representation like mesh and Gaussians, and synthesize motion from text or video control signals. However, dense mesh and Gaussian representations incur high computational costs and are prone to temporal artifacts, limiting animation quality and duration to only short clips. Meanwhile, text lacks fine-grained spatial and temporal details such as timing and coordination, while video entangles motion with appearance and background. Together, these limitations result in 4D animations that suffer from poor temporal consistency, wrong identification, and limited controllability. We address these issues with \texttt{ACT}, a trajectory-conditioned framework for topology-general skeletal animation. ACT uses skeletons as a compact structured and compute-efficient representation and 3D point trajectories from monocular video as explicit motion guidance which provide detailed motion patterns without appearance entanglement. At the core of ACT is a Routed Trajectory Injector, which achieves accurate and robust trajectory-to-joint transfer through three complementary designs: prior-guided hard routing establishes precise skeleton-to-mesh correspondences, global routing enables holistic joint-track interaction for full-body motion awareness, and local windowed cross-attention enforces fine-grained temporal alignment, improving micro-timing and reducing motion misalignment across varying motion rates. Extensive experiments demonstrate that \texttt{ACT} significantly outperforms existing methods in fidelity and temporal consistency.

AI.tldr: ACT introduces a trajectory-conditioned skeletal animation framework for 4D generation that uses skeletons as compact representations and monocular video 3D point trajectories for motion guidance, featuring a Routed Trajectory Injector for accurate trajectory-to-joint transfer.
AI.motivation: Existing 4D generation methods rely on dense mesh/Gaussian representations (high cost, temporal artifacts), text (lacks spatial/temporal details), or video (entangles motion with appearance), leading to poor temporal consistency, wrong identification, and limited controllability.
AI.method: ACT uses skeletons as compact structured representations and 3D point trajectories from monocular video as explicit motion guidance. Its core is a Routed Trajectory Injector with three components: prior-guided hard routing (precise skeleton-to-mesh correspondences), global routing (holistic joint-track interaction), and local windowed cross-attention (fine-grained temporal alignment).
AI.result: ACT significantly outperforms existing methods in fidelity and temporal consistency, enabling topology-general skeletal animation with improved motion alignment and reduced temporal artifacts.
AI.conclusion: ACT effectively addresses key limitations of prior 4D generation approaches through trajectory-conditioned skeletal animation, achieving better temporal consistency, accurate motion control, and longer animation duration.

title: Hypergraph Normal World Models for Logical Visual Anomaly Detection
summary: Visual anomaly detection is often deployed with only normal training images. Most one-class detectors map test patches or features to a normal reference distribution. This works well for local structural defects. Logical anomalies are different. Each visible part may look normal, while the whole image violates a normal count, co-occurrence, or spatial relation. This paper studies whether a model can learn such a category-specific normal world from nominal images alone. We propose the Hypergraph Normal World Model, a normal-only detector that distills frozen DINOv2 patch tokens into patch, relation, and hypergraph statistics. It builds spatial hyperedges over token groups. It then scores each test image with an information quotient that separates local, relational, hyperedge, and hyperedge-relation evidence. On the available MVTec LOCO breakfast-box validation data, the full hypergraph model improves logical anomaly AUROC from 0.8434 for DINOv2 patch-kNN to 0.9279. It also improves over the non-hypergraph variant, from 0.9013 to 0.9279. Few-shot experiments show that the model remains effective with very limited normal images. We also test whether the score reflects normal-world knowledge rather than a shallow mapping. t-SNE separates logical anomalies in the learned energy space. Relation counterfactuals increase the information quotient by 83.13 on average. Random hypergraphs reduce logical AUROC, and hyperedge attribution is much larger on logical anomalies. Qualitative examples show that high scores are driven by relation-bearing terms. These results suggest that logical visual anomaly detection should model normal relations, not only normal local patches.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a Hypergraph Normal World Model for logical anomaly detection that uses frozen DINOv2 patch tokens and builds spatial hyperedges to model normal relations, achieving significant AUROC improvements on MVTec LOCO data (0.9279 vs 0.8434 for patch-kNN).
AI.motivation: Standard one-class anomaly detectors work well for local structural defects but fail on logical anomalies where individual parts appear normal but violate normal counts, co-occurrences, or spatial relations. This work investigates whether a model can learn category-specific normal relations from only normal images.
AI.method: The Hypergraph Normal World Model distills frozen DINOv2 patch tokens into patch, relation, and hypergraph statistics. It constructs spatial hyperedges over token groups and scores each test image using an information quotient that separates local, relational, hyperedge, and hyperedge-relation evidence.
AI.result: On MVTec LOCO breakfast-box validation data, logical anomaly AUROC improved from 0.8434 (DINOv2 patch-kNN) to 0.9279, and from 0.9013 (non-hypergraph variant) to 0.9279. t-SNE separates logical anomalies in the energy space. Relation counterfactuals increase information quotient by 83.13 on average.
AI.conclusion: Logical visual anomaly detection should model normal relations, not only normal local patches. The hypergraph structure captures relational constraints that are essential for detecting logical anomalies.

title: Beyond Visual Forensics: Auditing Multimodal Robustness for Synthetic Medical Image Detection
summary: With the rapid adoption of generative AI, synthetic medical images pose growing risks, including diagnostic deception and insurance fraud. Although prior work has explored vision-language model (VLM)-based synthetic image detection, these evaluations typically consider images in isolation. In clinical practice, however, images are interpreted alongside structured records and metadata, and VLMs are increasingly deployed under joint image-record inputs. We uncover a previously underexamined multimodal vulnerability: when given both modalities, VLMs may overweight record context in authenticity judgments, such that the same image receives different predictions solely due to changes in its accompanying text. This raises concerns about robustness in real-world deployment. To systematically characterize this effect, we reformulate synthetic medical image detection as an audit of multimodal robustness at the image-record interface and introduce a paired benchmark that holds the image fixed while swapping controlled metadata variants. Across multiple imaging modalities, we evaluate diverse open-weight and frontier API VLMs and quantify how metadata alone shifts authenticity predictions. Our benchmark provides a standardized tool for assessing and improving multimodal robustness beyond image-only settings. The code is available at https://github.com/chiuhaohao/Beyond-Visual-Forensics.

AI.tldr: This paper reveals that VLMs over-rely on accompanying text metadata when judging synthetic medical image authenticity, introducing a new multimodal vulnerability. The authors propose a benchmark to evaluate this robustness issue across different imaging modalities and models.
AI.motivation: Synthetic medical image detection is typically studied in image-only settings, but in real clinical practice, VLMs process both images and structured records/metadata. The paper uncovers that metadata alone can shift authenticity predictions even when the image is fixed, posing robustness and safety concerns.
AI.method: The authors reformulate synthetic medical image detection as an audit of multimodal robustness, introducing a paired benchmark that holds the image fixed while swapping controlled metadata variants. They evaluate diverse open-weight and frontier API VLMs across multiple imaging modalities.
AI.result: VLMs may overweight record context in authenticity judgments, where the same image receives different predictions solely due to changes in its accompanying text. This reveals a previously underexamined multimodal vulnerability.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark provides a standardized tool for assessing and improving multimodal robustness beyond image-only settings, addressing critical deployment safety concerns.

title: Transferable Attack against Face Swapping in an Extended Space
summary: Although deep Face Swapping (FS) models may benefit the entertainment industry, they pose severe threats to privacy and security. Existing protections, including deepfake detection and adversarial perturbation, are either passive responses or ineffective to unseen subject-agnostic FS models. In this paper, we propose a transferable attack against subject-agnostic FS models named Additive Identity attack based on a Relighting function (AIR). AIR leverages reillumination and additive perturbations to mislead the identity extraction modules in subject-agnostic FS models. By using these two types of perturbations simultaneously, the attack space is extended such that stronger but more visually natural adversarial examples can be identified. To further enhance the visual quality while preserving the effectiveness of the attack, an adaptive translation-invariant operation and an illumination control scheme are designed for AIR. Unlike other methods, AIR does not require a surrogate FS model to achieve high transferability. In addition, a mathematical proof is given for the extension of the attack space. Extensive experiments using 1000 image pairs across various state-of-the-art subject-agnostic FS models, including GAN and diffusion-based FS models, show that AIR surpasses all existing attacks in terms of both attack success rate and image quality.

AI.tldr: AIR proposes a transferable attack using relighting and additive perturbations to fool subject-agnostic face swapping models, achieving high attack success rate and image quality without needing a surrogate model.
AI.motivation: Deep face swapping models threaten privacy and security; existing protections (detection, adversarial perturbations) are either passive or ineffective against unseen subject-agnostic FS models.
AI.method: AIR combines reillumination and additive perturbations to mislead identity extraction modules, uses adaptive translation-invariant operation and illumination control scheme to enhance visual quality, and provides mathematical proof for attack space extension.
AI.result: Evaluated on 1000 image pairs across GAN and diffusion-based FS models; AIR surpasses all existing attacks in both attack success rate and image quality.
AI.conclusion: AIR is an effective, transferable defense against subject-agnostic FS models without requiring a surrogate model, opening a new direction for proactive protection against face swapping.

title: Anatomically-conditioned Latent Diffusion Model for Data-Efficient Few-Shot Cross-Domain 3D Glioma MRI Synthesis
summary: Accurate classification of diffuse gliomas is often hindered by domain shifts across centers and a lack of large, annotated datasets. We propose the Anatomically-conditioned Latent Diffusion Model (ALDM), a novel framework for data-efficient, few-shot 3D volumetric MRI synthesis. ALDM utilizes a two-stage approach: a 3D variational autoencoder learns anatomical priors from a data-rich source domain, while a conditional latent diffusion model, guided by tumor masks via a ControlNet, generates structurally coherent volumes for a data-scarce target domain. Evaluated in an extreme few-shot setting with only 16 target images, ALDM outperformed GAN and hybrid baselines, achieving a superior Frechet Inception Distance (FID) of 85.40 and a downstream classification AUC of 0.987. Qualitative results confirm that the model preserves sharp pathology boundaries and cross-modal consistency, with visual fidelity improving progressively during training. By capturing essential diagnostic features, ALDM provides a robust tool for clinical data augmentation in low-resource settings. Our implementation is available at https://github.com/Analytics-Everywhere-Lab/anatomically-conditioned-LDM.

AI.tldr: ALDM is a two-stage diffusion model that generates synthetic 3D MRI volumes to improve glioma classification in data-scarce settings, achieving strong results with only 16 target images.
AI.motivation: Accurate glioma classification is hindered by domain shifts across centers and a lack of large, annotated datasets, especially in low-resource clinical settings.
AI.method: A two-stage framework: (1) a 3D VAE learns anatomical priors from a source domain; (2) a conditional latent diffusion model with ControlNet guidance generates target-domain volumes conditioned on tumor masks.
AI.result: With only 16 target images, ALDM achieved FID of 85.40 and downstream classification AUC of 0.987, outperforming GAN and hybrid baselines.
AI.conclusion: ALDM is a robust tool for data augmentation in low-resource settings, preserving sharp pathology boundaries and cross-modal consistency for reliable glioma classification.

title: Teach-to-Reason: Competition-Guided Reasoning with a Self-Improving Teacher
summary: Chest X-ray visual question answering (CXR VQA) requires models not only to predict correct answers, but also to produce reliable medical reasoning. However, existing reinforcement-learning-based training typically relies on answer-level rewards, which are often too coarse to improve chain-of-thought (CoT) quality and can become ineffective when group-level advantages collapse to zero. We propose \textbf{Teach-to-Reason (T2R)}, a framework that introduces comparison-based supervision into CoT optimization through a self-improving \emph{Teacher} and a competition-guided \emph{Reasoner}. As the Teacher is iteratively strengthened via self-competition, the Reasoner is optimized against progressively stronger Teacher-generated references. We further introduce a case-wise reward design that preserves the original reward-induced positive/negative partition when it is informative, and restores supervision from competition scores when the original reward signal degenerates. Experiments on multiple CXR open-ended VQA benchmarks show that T2R consistently outperforms strong baselines, indicating that comparison-based supervision, when integrated in a controlled and principled manner, provides a more effective training signal for reasoning optimization.

AI.tldr: T2R improves CXR VQA reasoning by using comparison-based supervision via a self-improving Teacher and competition-guided Reasoner, outperforming standard RL methods with coarse answer-level rewards.
AI.motivation: Existing RL-based training for CXR VQA uses coarse answer-level rewards that fail to improve chain-of-thought quality and become ineffective when group-level advantages collapse to zero.
AI.method: T2R introduces a self-improving Teacher (strengthened via self-competition) and a competition-guided Reasoner (optimized against progressively stronger Teacher references), plus a case-wise reward design that preserves informative reward signals and restores supervision from competition scores when rewards degenerate.
AI.result: T2R consistently outperforms strong baselines on multiple CXR open-ended VQA benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Comparison-based supervision, integrated in a controlled and principled manner, provides a more effective training signal for reasoning optimization in CXR VQA.

title: Gastroendoscopy View Synthesis: A New Real Dataset and Evaluation
summary: Novel view synthesis (NVS) is an active research topic in computer vision, owing to the success of neural radiance field (NeRF) and 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS) methods. While NVS opens the door to potential applications in gastroendoscopy, such as extending the field of view of endoscopic images and enabling digital twins for 3D archiving and endoscopist manipulation training, the dataset is insufficient to evaluate NVS for gastroendoscopy. In this paper, we present the first real gastroscopy dataset for NVS, namely the GastroNVS dataset, which contains a set of gastroscopic images, camera poses, and a point cloud for real gastroendoscopy inspection. To assess the suitability of the GastroNVS dataset, we evaluate several 3DGS methods and discuss the challenges for future development. The dataset is available on request from our project page.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the first real gastroscopy dataset (GastroNVS) for novel view synthesis, containing images, camera poses, and point clouds, and evaluates 3DGS methods to benchmark NVS in gastroendoscopy.
AI.motivation: Novel view synthesis has potential applications in gastroendoscopy (e.g., extending field of view, digital twins, training), but existing datasets are insufficient to evaluate NVS for this domain.
AI.method: The authors construct the GastroNVS dataset comprising real gastroscopic images, camera poses, and point clouds, then evaluate multiple 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) methods on this dataset.
AI.result: The GastroNVS dataset is presented as the first real gastroscopy NVS dataset, and evaluations of 3DGS methods reveal challenges specific to gastroendoscopy.
AI.conclusion: The GastroNVS dataset fills a critical gap for NVS research in gastroendoscopy, and future work should address the identified challenges.

title: PRISM: Feed-Forward Single-Image 3D Reconstruction via Geometric Warp-Residual Modeling
summary: Reconstructing 3D scenes from a single image is a fundamental challenge in computer vision, with broad applications in virtual reality, robotics, and content creation. Recent methods achieve outstanding performance by leveraging camera-controlled video diffusion models, but rely on iterative diffusion sampling, which greatly limits their practical deployment. We observe that geometric forward warping alone can cover the majority of a target view directly from the input image, with only a compact residual left for the encoder to correct. Motivated by this observation, we propose PRISM, a feed-forward framework that decomposes multi-view latent prediction into a parameter-free geometric prior and a learned residual correction, with no diffusion sampling required at inference. To enable generalization from purely synthetic training data, we devise a two-stage training strategy combining latents supervised distillation for geometric generalization and perceptual fine-tuning for appearance quality optimization. Extensive experiments on three benchmarks demonstrate that PRISM achieves competitive reconstruction quality compared with diffusion-based methods, while reducing inference time dramatically to only 36 seconds per scene.

AI.tldr: PRISM is a feed-forward framework for single-image 3D scene reconstruction that decomposes multi-view latent prediction into geometric forward warping and learned residual correction, eliminating the need for iterative diffusion sampling at inference. It achieves competitive quality while reducing inference time to 36 seconds per scene.
AI.motivation: Existing camera-controlled video diffusion models for 3D reconstruction rely on iterative diffusion sampling, which limits practical deployment due to high inference cost. The authors observe that geometric forward warping can already cover most of a target view directly from the input, leaving only a compact residual for the encoder to handle.
AI.method: PRISM is a feed-forward framework with two key components: (1) parameter-free geometric prior via forward warping, and (2) learned residual correction. A two-stage training strategy is used: latent supervised distillation for geometric generalization, followed by perceptual fine-tuning for appearance quality optimization, enabling generalization from purely synthetic data.
AI.result: Across three benchmarks, PRISM achieves reconstruction quality competitive with diffusion-based methods while dramatically reducing inference time to only 36 seconds per scene, compared to the iterative sampling process required by prior approaches.
AI.conclusion: PRISM demonstrates that diffusion sampling can be eliminated for single-image 3D reconstruction by leveraging geometric priors with residual correction, offering a practical balance between quality and efficiency suitable for real-world deployment.

title: LinStereo: Linear-Complexity Global Attention for Multi-Scale Iterative Stereo Matching
summary: Existing Vision Foundation Model (VFM)-based iterative stereo pipelines under-exploit three information pathways: multi-scale backbone features are collapsed into single-level correlations, geometric priors remain untapped at initialization, and context propagates only locally. These gaps widen under degraded photometric cues, making underwater scenes a stringent generalization test. To address this, we propose LinStereo, built upon Depth Anything V3, whose core is a Position-Aware Linear Attention (PALA) module that replaces local recurrence with global aggregation at linear cost, propagating reliable estimates from well-matched regions into degraded areas while preserving disparity structure. PALA is made effective by two enabling components: Hierarchical Semantic Cost Volumes (HSCV), which supply scale-aligned correlations from the VFM feature hierarchy, and a Depth Prior Initialization (DPI) that converts monocular depth into a metrically calibrated warm start. LinStereo achieves state-of-the-art-level accuracy on standard benchmarks and strong cross-domain generalization, particularly on underwater scene where severe photometric degradation makes stereo matching particularly challenging, attaining the best overall accuracy with consistent gains 28% lower AbsRel on TartanAir-UW, 26% on SQUID, a real-world underwater dataset).

AI.tldr: LinStereo improves stereo matching by enhancing three under-exploited information pathways in VFM-based pipelines, using Position-Aware Linear Attention (PALA), Hierarchical Semantic Cost Volumes (HSCV), and Depth Prior Initialization (DPI), achieving state-of-the-art accuracy especially on underwater scenes.
AI.motivation: Existing VFM-based stereo pipelines under-utilize multi-scale features (collapsed into single-level correlations), geometric priors (unused at initialization), and context (local only). These issues worsen under degraded photometric cues like underwater scenes, motivating a more robust approach.
AI.method: LinStereo builds on Depth Anything V3, introducing: (1) Position-Aware Linear Attention (PALA) for global aggregation at linear cost, (2) Hierarchical Semantic Cost Volumes (HSCV) for scale-aligned correlations from VFM features, and (3) Depth Prior Initialization (DPI) converting monocular depth into a metrically calibrated warm start.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on standard benchmarks and strong cross-domain generalization. On underwater scenes: 28% lower AbsRel on TartanAir-UW, 26% lower on SQUID (real-world underwater dataset).
AI.conclusion: LinSterero effectively addresses key information pathway limitations in stereo matching, showing particular strength in challenging underwater environments with severe photometric degradation.

title: C3-Bench: A Context-Aware Change Captioning Benchmark
summary: While Change Captioning systems have garnered substantial attention to respond to our evolving world, their true performance on diverse real-world change contexts remains largely unexplored due to the lack of comprehensive evaluation frameworks. To fill this gap, we propose C3-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating Context-aware Change Captioning. C3-Bench features: (1) 4,996 human-labeled image pairs of 51 real-world change contexts across four domains (e.g., natural scenes, remote sensing imagery, image editing, and anomalies), each with diverse, carefully curated scenarios derived from multiple change-centric communities; and (2) the first LLM-as-Judge evaluation framework in the change captioning task that measure fine-grained dimensions (e.g., correctness, specificity, fluency, and relevance), along with a novel reversibility metric exploring whether models understand changes with symmetric consistency. Based on C3-Bench, we benchmark 32 models -- including conventional change captioning models, proprietary Large Multimodal Models (LMMs), and 2B-90B open-source LMMs. We reveal a fundamental blind spot in the prevailing change captioning paradigm: Once the change context departs from training-style regimes, conventional models collapse, and even state-of-the-art LMMs such as GPT-5.2 exhibit systematic domain- and position-dependent errors that distort reliable change understanding. By making these hidden failure modes explicit and measurable, we delineate the next frontier for building generalizable and trustworthy change captioning systems. All codes and datasets are publicly available on the project page.

AI.tldr: C3-Bench is a comprehensive benchmark for Context-aware Change Captioning, featuring 4,996 human-labeled image pairs across 51 real-world change contexts, an LLM-as-Judge evaluation framework, and a novel reversibility metric. Benchmarking 32 models reveals that conventional models collapse on diverse contexts and even top LMMs like GPT-5.2 show systematic domain- and position-dependent errors.
AI.motivation: Existing Change Captioning systems lack comprehensive evaluation frameworks, making their true performance on diverse real-world change contexts largely unexplored.
AI.method: The authors propose C3-Bench with 4,996 human-labeled image pairs across 51 change contexts (4 domains), an LLM-as-Judge evaluation framework measuring correctness, specificity, fluency, and relevance, plus a reversibility metric for symmetric consistency.
AI.result: Benchmarked 32 models; conventional models collapse when change contexts deviate from training-style regimes; even GPT-5.2 exhibits systematic domain- and position-dependent errors.
AI.conclusion: The paper identifies a fundamental blind spot in current change captioning paradigms and delineates the next frontier for building generalizable and trustworthy change captioning systems.

title: EchoStyle: Unlocking High-Fidelity Video Stylization with Reverse Data Synthesis
summary: While image stylization has been studied extensively, video stylization remains a critical and largely unsolved challenge in the field of intelligent content creation. Existing methods, usually utilizing a reference image as the style prior, suffer from content leakage, data scarcity and limited adaptability to long videos, leading to suboptimal results with severe style drift and motion distortion. For these issues, we present EchoStyle, a scalable text-driven framework to achieve high-quality stylization of videos with arbitrary lengths. To start with, we construct a video-to-video architecture to appropriately re-fuse the video content and the text style. To address data scarcity, we pioneer an automatic reverse-synthesis pipeline to establish V-Style20k, a large-scale stylization dataset of 20k high-quality video pairs. To facilitate long video stylization, we devise an init-follow-mode mechanism along with a sliding-window inference strategy. Extensive experiments demonstrate EchoStyle's excellent performance across a wide range of artistic styles, even comparable to leading closed-source solutions.

AI.tldr: EchoStyle is a scalable text-driven video stylization framework that addresses content leakage, data scarcity, and long-video adaptability issues through a video-to-video architecture, an automatic reverse-synthesis pipeline creating the V-Style20k dataset (20k video pairs), and an init-follow-mode mechanism with sliding-window inference, achieving high-quality results comparable to leading solutions.
AI.motivation: Video stylization remains a largely unsolved challenge. Existing reference-image-based methods suffer from content leakage, data scarcity, and limited adaptability to long videos, causing style drift and motion distortion.
AI.method: 1) Video-to-video architecture for re-fusing video content and text style. 2) Automatic reverse-synthesis pipeline to create V-Style20k dataset (20k video pairs). 3) Init-follow-mode mechanism with sliding-window inference for long video stylization.
AI.result: EchoStyle achieves excellent performance across a wide range of artistic styles, even comparable to leading closed-source solutions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively solves key challenges in video stylization (content leakage, data scarcity, long-video adaptability) and produces high-quality results competitive with state-of-the-art closed-source methods.

title: Causal-rCM: A Unified Teacher-Forcing and Self-Forcing Open Recipe for Autoregressive Diffusion Distillation in Streaming Video Generation and Interactive World Models
summary:
Autoregressive video diffusion with causal diffusion transformers has emerged as a major paradigm for real-time streaming video generation and action-conditioned interactive world models. In this work, we extend rCM, an advanced diffusion distillation framework, to autoregressive video diffusion. The core philosophy of rCM lies in the complementarity between forward and reverse divergences, represented by consistency models (CMs) and distribution matching distillation (DMD), respectively, in diffusion distillation. This philosophy naturally carries over to the autoregressive setting, where teacher-forcing (TF) provides an offline, forward-divergence causal training paradigm, while self-forcing (SF) corresponds to an on-policy, reverse-divergence refinement.
  Our contributions are: (1) through extensive experiments, we show that teacher-forcing CM is currently the best complement to self-forcing DMD as an initialization strategy (2) we present the first implementation of teacher-forcing-based continuous-time CMs (e.g., sCM/MeanFlow) for autoregressive video diffusion, enabled by our custom-mask FlashAttention-2 JVP kernel, achieving 10$\times$ faster convergence compared to discrete-time CMs (dCMs) (3) we introduce Causal-rCM, a leading, unified, and scalable algorithm-infrastructure open recipe for diffusion distillation and causal training (4) we achieve state-of-the-art streaming video generation performance in both frame-wise and chunk-wise settings, using only synthetic data for training.
  Notably, our distilled 2-step causal Wan2.1-1.3B model achieves a VBench-T2V score of 84.63 with only 1 or 2 sampling steps. We further apply Causal-rCM to Cosmos 3, an advanced omnimodal world foundation model for physical AI with action-conditioned generation capability, enabling an interactive world model.

AI.tldr: This paper extends the rCM diffusion distillation framework to autoregressive video diffusion, introducing Causal-rCM that combines teacher-forcing consistency models with self-forcing distribution matching distillation. It achieves state-of-the-art streaming video generation with up to 10x faster convergence and scores 84.63 on VBench-T2V using only 1-2 sampling steps.
AI.motivation: To extend the rCM diffusion distillation framework (which leverages the complementarity between forward divergence from consistency models and reverse divergence from distribution matching distillation) to the autoregressive video diffusion setting, enabling efficient real-time streaming video generation and interactive world models.
AI.method: The paper proposes Causal-rCM by: (1) combining teacher-forcing CM (forward divergence) with self-forcing DMD (reverse divergence) as complementary training strategies; (2) implementing teacher-forcing-based continuous-time CMs (sCM/MeanFlow) using a custom-mask FlashAttention-2 JVP kernel for 10x faster convergence over discrete-time CMs; (3) applying this framework to both frame-wise and chunk-wise autoregressive video diffusion settings.
AI.result: Distilled 2-step causal Wan2.1-1.3B model achieves VBench-T2V score of 84.63 with only 1-2 sampling steps. The approach achieves state-of-the-art streaming video generation performance using only synthetic data, and is successfully applied to Cosmos 3 omnimodal world foundation model for interactive world modeling.
AI.conclusion: Teacher-forcing CM serves as the best complement to self-forcing DMD as an initialization strategy. The proposed Causal-rCM provides a unified, scalable open recipe for diffusion distillation and causal training, achieving SOTA streaming video generation with minimal sampling steps.

title: TACO: Towards Task-Consistent Open-Vocabulary Adaptation in Video Recognition
summary: Adapting CLIP for open-vocabulary video recognition necessitates a delicate balance between newly acquired video knowledge and the pretrained generalization. While existing studies pursue this generalization-specialization trade-off with additional regularizations or constraints, we argue that they overlook the deviation of representations beyond the fine-tuning data distribution, resulting in suboptimal adaptation effects. We believe such deviation is inherited from the inconsistency between the fine-tuning and evaluation objectives, where model optimization is restricted to the known training distribution but evaluated on unseen ones. In this paper, we introduce \emph{TACO}, a simple yet effective framework to mitigate the potential negative effects induced by this inconsistency. Our key insight is that adaptation should preserve OOD-relevant alignment beyond the training distribution. To this end, we propose \emph{Relative Structure Distillation}, which regularizes the relative geometry of the representation space and suppresses harmful alignment shift during training. We further decouple the representation space from the optimization space with a lightweight specialization projection, allowing task-specific adaptation without directly overspecializing the representations used at test time. \emph{TACO} establishes state-of-the-art performance on diverse benchmarks under cross-dataset and base-to-novel settings. Code will be released at https://github.com/ZMHH-H/TACO.

AI.tldr: TACO adapts CLIP for open-vocabulary video recognition by using Relative Structure Distillation to preserve OOD-relevant alignment and a lightweight specialization projection to decouple representation from optimization, achieving SOTA results.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for adapting CLIP to video overlook representation deviation beyond the fine-tuning data distribution due to inconsistency between training and evaluation objectives, leading to suboptimal adaptation.
AI.method: Two key components: (1) Relative Structure Distillation - regularizes relative geometry of the representation space to suppress harmful alignment shift; (2) Lightweight specialization projection - decouples representation space from optimization space to avoid overspecializing test-time representations.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on diverse benchmarks under cross-dataset and base-to-novel settings.
AI.conclusion: TACO effectively balances generalization and specialization for open-vocabulary video recognition by preserving OOD-relevant alignment and decoupling representation from task-specific optimization.

title: Cross-View Variance Correlation in Path-Traced Stereo:A Hidden Shortcut in Synthetic Training Data
summary: Path-traced synthetic stereo data underlie a large fraction of modern disparity-estimation training pipelines. We report a previously unrecognised property of such data: while the Monte Carlo (MC) noise streams of the two cameras are statistically independent, the underlying \emph{variance fields} -- deterministic per-pixel functions of the rendering integrand -- are highly correlated once aligned by the ground-truth disparity warp. Across 20 scenes rendered with Mitsuba~3, the warped Pearson correlation reaches $ρ{=}0.754{\pm}0.016$ across 20 scenes at $\mathrm{SPP}{=}512$, and on a representative scene remains essentially invariant ($ρ{=}0.778{\pm}0.001$) over a $16\times$ range of samples per pixel. The effect is strongest in Lambertian regions ($ρ{\approx}0.78$) and substantially weaker in glass ($ρ{\approx}0.30$), as predicted by an integrand decomposition into view-independent and view-dependent components. A residual-shuffle intervention that breaks the cross-view alignment while preserving the clean image degrades the GT cost margin by $33\%$ on non-glass and the variance-based winner-take-all accuracy on glass by $4.3\times$, confirming the structure functions as a matching cue. This signal is unique to MC-rendered data and constitutes a candidate sim-to-real shortcut whose impact on trained networks remains to be quantified.

AI.tldr: Path-traced synthetic stereo data exhibit highly correlated variance fields across left/right views when aligned by ground-truth disparity, creating an unintended matching cue that could serve as a sim-to-real shortcut for disparity networks.
AI.motivation: To identify and characterize a previously unrecognized property of Monte Carlo-rendered stereo data: correlated noise variance fields across views, which may act as an unintended shortcut signal for learned disparity estimation.
AI.method: Analyzed 20 scenes rendered in Mitsuba~3 at SPP=512, measured warped Pearson correlation of variance fields. Decomposed integrand into view-dependent/independent components to predict material-specific effects. Tested a residual-shuffle intervention breaking cross-view variance alignment.
AI.result: Warped Pearson correlation ρ=0.754±0.016 across 20 scenes at SPP=512; invariant over 16× SPP range (ρ=0.778±0.001). Lambertian regions show ρ≈0.78, glass regions ρ≈0.30. Residual shuffle degrades GT cost margin by 33% on non-glass and variance-based WTA accuracy on glass by 4.3×.
AI.conclusion: MC-rendered stereo data contain correlated variance fields that function as a matching cue unique to synthetic data, constituting a candidate sim-to-real shortcut whose impact on trained disparity networks requires further study.

title: HG-Bench: A Benchmark for Multi-Page Handwritten Answer-Region Grounding in Automated Homework Assessment
summary: Automated homework assessment depends not only on recognizing student answers, but also on accurately locating where each answer and each intermediate reasoning step appears in noisy, multi-page handwritten work. This paper addresses the missing evaluation setting of page-aware, two-level answer-region grounding: given a sequence of homework page images, a model must localize complete answer regions and their ordered step-level subregions. We introduce HG-Bench, a benchmark of 500 human-annotated K-12 homework samples curated from a 1,489,278-image source pool, with question-level and step-level boxes linked by a hierarchical containment constraint. HG-Bench is paired with a page-aware evaluation protocol that separately measures complete-answer localization (FA) and step-level decomposition (FSm), revealing whether models truly ground the spatial structure of student reasoning rather than merely parse visible text. Across frontier closed-source APIs and competitive open-weight VLMs, no zero-shot system exceeds 55.22% on FA or 48.22% on FSm, while a GLM-4.6V 9B reference model fine-tuned on ~10k in-domain examples reaches 74.97/72.26. These results identify step-level handwritten grounding as a concrete capability gap and provide a reproducible benchmark, evaluation protocol, and trained reference point for future work on automated homework assessment.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces HG-Bench, a benchmark for page-aware, two-level answer-region grounding in multi-page handwritten homework. Current vision-language models perform poorly on this task (≤55.22% FA, ≤48.22% FSm), while a fine-tuned model reaches 74.97/72.26, revealing a capability gap in step-level handwritten reasoning grounding.
AI.motivation: Existing automated homework assessment systems lack a benchmark for evaluating page-aware, two-level answer-region grounding—localizing both complete answers and their ordered step-level subregions across noisy multi-page handwritten work.
AI.method: The authors curate HG-Bench (500 human-annotated K-12 samples from 1,489,278 images) with hierarchical containment constraints linking question-level and step-level boxes. They propose a page-aware evaluation protocol measuring complete-answer localization (FA) and step-level decomposition (FSm).
AI.result: Zero-shot VLMs score ≤55.22% on FA and ≤48.22% on FSm. GLM-4.6V 9B fine-tuned on ~10k in-domain examples achieves 74.97/72.26.
AI.conclusion: Step-level handwritten grounding is a concrete capability gap in current VLMs, and HG-Bench provides a reproducible benchmark, evaluation protocol, and reference model for future automated homework assessment research.

title: C2RM-Seg: Causal Counterfactual Reasoning with Structural-Semantic Priors for Weakly Supervised Histopathological Tissue Segmentation
comment: 11 pages, 3 figures. Code is available at https://github.com/OceanPetal/C2AM-Seg
summary: Histopathological tissue segmentation is essential for computer-aided diagnosis, yet weakly supervised methods often suffer from noisy pseudo-labels generated by Class Activation Mapping (CAM). Existing CAM approaches tend to focus on staining-driven appearance cues rather than true causal tissue morphology, resulting in spurious localization and poor structural consistency. To address this issue, we propose C$^2$RM-Seg, a two-stage framework that integrates causal pseudo-label refinement with structure-aware semantic enhancement. For classification, we introduce a Causal Counterfactual Reasoning Module (C$^2$RM) that decomposes features into latent factors and performs counterfactual intervention via a learned causal structure matrix, suppressing confounding context and producing morphology-aligned CAMs. For segmentation, we design a Dual-Path Structural-Semantic Architecture that combines fine-grained structural features from ResNeSt with global semantic priors from a frozen DINOV3 foundation model. A cross-path gating mechanism adaptively regulates semantic injection using local structural cues to preserve boundary fidelity. To further mitigate residual pseudo-label noise, we propose an Uncertainty-Gated Margin (UGM) loss, which dynamically balances margin enforcement and confidence learning based on prediction uncertainty. Extensive experiments on two public histopathological tissue datasets show that C$^2$RM-Seg achieves state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: C$^2$RM-Seg is a two-stage weakly supervised histopathological segmentation framework that uses causal counterfactual reasoning to refine CAM-based pseudo-labels and a dual-path architecture combining ResNeSt and DINOV3 features with uncertainty-gated loss, achieving SOTA on two public datasets.
AI.motivation: Weakly supervised histopathological segmentation suffers from noisy pseudo-labels because existing CAM methods focus on staining-driven appearance cues rather than true causal tissue morphology, leading to spurious localization and poor structural consistency.
AI.method: A two-stage framework: (1) Causal Counterfactual Reasoning Module (C$^2$RM) decomposes features into latent factors and performs counterfactual intervention via a learned causal structure matrix to produce morphology-aligned CAMs; (2) Dual-Path Structural-Semantic Architecture combining ResNeSt (fine-grained structure) and frozen DINOV3 (global semantics) with cross-path gating mechanism, trained with Uncertainty-Gated Margin (UGM) loss.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on two public histopathological tissue datasets.
AI.conclusion: Causal reasoning and structural-semantic fusion effectively mitigate pseudo-label noise in weakly supervised histopathological segmentation, outperforming existing methods.

title: PatchINR: Patch-Based Implicit Neural Representations for Efficient and Scalable Inference
summary: Implicit Neural Representation (INR) provides an effective approach for continuous signal modeling, but classical per-pixel inference results in quadratic growth in inference count, leading to dramatically increased computational costs in high-resolution application scenarios. To address this issue, we propose a patch-based approach that treats non-overlapping patches as fundamental processing units and predicts entire pixel patches in a single forward pass, significantly reducing the number of inference queries required. To validate the effectiveness of our approach, we propose a hardware acceleration architecture on the Field Programmable Gate Array (FPGA) platform for the INR model, which features a configurable pipeline and supports dual-precision computation. Our patch-based INR achieves comparable reconstruction quality to pixel-level INR (34.97 dB PSNR with 2 x 2 patches) while reducing inference latency by 75% with only 0.6% parameter overhead.

AI.tldr: A patch-based Implicit Neural Representation (INR) approach that processes non-overlapping patches in a single forward pass, reducing inference latency by 75% with minimal quality loss (34.97 dB PSNR), accelerated by an FPGA hardware architecture with configurable pipeline and dual-precision support.
AI.motivation: Traditional per-pixel INR inference causes quadratic growth in computational costs at high resolutions, limiting practical deployment.
AI.method: Proposes patch-based INR treating non-overlapping patches as fundamental units, predicting entire patches in one forward pass; implements FPGA acceleration with configurable pipeline and dual-precision computation.
AI.result: Achieves 34.97 dB PSNR (2x2 patches), 75% inference latency reduction, with only 0.6% parameter overhead.
AI.conclusion: Patch-based INR significantly improves computational efficiency for high-resolution applications while maintaining reconstruction quality, with practical FPGA acceleration.

title: Spatio-Temporal Mixture-of-Modality-Experts Diffusion for Quantitative DCE-MRI Synthesis from Incomplete MR Sequences
summary: Quantitative maps from dynamic contrast-enhanced MRI (DCE-MRI) are essential for tumor assessment but are often unavailable due to contrast-agent risks and protocol variability. Prior methods predict these maps from other MRI modalities, yet most assume fixed, fully observed inputs and fail under realistic missingness. We present Spatio-Temporal Mixture-of-Modality-Experts (ST-MoME), a conditional diffusion framework that synthesizes 3D DCE parameter maps from diverse subsets of multimodal MRI. ST-MoME fuses modality-specific expert features through a spatio-temporal gating network that produces voxel-wise, timestep-dependent weights, forming a conditioning tensor that guides denoising. To preserve quantitative fidelity, ST-MoME performs diffusion directly in image space with 3D patch-based training and a Swin-based backbone. On a clinical brain-tumor cohort of 386 patients, we evaluate ST-MoME across 16 controlled modality-availability scenarios. It achieves the lowest mean Normalized Mean Square Error (NMSE) aggregated across all three DCE parameters, with leading performance on $v_p$ and $v_e$, competitive results on $K^{\mathrm{trans}}$, and the lowest reconstruction error within the clinically critical tumor region. A post-hoc analysis of the learned gating dynamics shows a structural-early, physiological-late fusion schedule consistent with clinical intuition.

AI.tldr: ST-MoME is a conditional diffusion framework that synthesizes 3D DCE-MRI parameter maps from arbitrary subsets of multimodal MRI, using a spatio-temporal mixture-of-modality-experts approach with voxel-wise gating, achieving state-of-the-art results on a 386-patient brain tumor cohort across 16 missing-modality scenarios.
AI.motivation: DCE-MRI quantitative maps are clinically valuable for tumor assessment but often unavailable due to contrast-agent risks and protocol variability. Existing prediction methods assume fixed, fully observed inputs and fail under realistic missing-modality scenarios.
AI.method: ST-MoME uses a conditional diffusion framework with modality-specific expert features fused via a spatio-temporal gating network that produces voxel-wise, timestep-dependent weights. Diffusion is performed directly in image space with 3D patch-based training and a Swin Transformer backbone.
AI.result: On 386 brain-tumor patients across 16 modality-availability scenarios, ST-MoME achieves the lowest mean NMSE aggregated across all three DCE parameters, leading on v_p and v_e, competitive on K^{trans}, and lowest reconstruction error in tumor regions.
AI.conclusion: ST-MoME effectively handles realistic missing-modality scenarios for DCE parameter map synthesis, and its learned gating dynamics reveal a clinically intuitive structural-early, physiological-late fusion schedule.

title: SAC$^2$-Net: Semantic Anchoring and Complementary-Consensus Fusion for Multimodal Micro-Expression Recognition
summary: Micro-expression recognition (MER) is challenging due to subtle facial movements, limited data, and the ambiguous relationship between Action Units (AUs) and emotion categories. Optical flow and motion magnification have been widely used to describe subtle facial dynamics from different perspectives: the former captures local motion displacement, while the latter amplifies weak appearance changes. In this work, we observe that these two modalities often exhibit asymmetric failure patterns: one modality may become noisy, distorted, or uninformative, while the other still preserves discriminative AU-related evidence. This phenomenon reveals their complementarity, but also raises two key challenges for fusion: cross-modal heterogeneity and spatially varying modality reliability. Motivated by this observation, we propose SAC$^2$-Net, a Semantic Anchoring and Complementary-Consensus Network for multimodal MER, which first aligns visual modalities with semantic anchors and then performs reliability-aware fusion. To reduce cross-modal heterogeneity before fusion, we introduce Semantic Anchoring Soft Alignment (SASA), which converts activated AUs into textual prompts and uses them as stable semantic anchors to align motion-magnified and optical-flow representations. Unlike hard contrastive learning, SASA constructs hierarchical AU-aware soft labels to preserve semantic proximity among samples with overlapping or anatomically related AU patterns. Based on the aligned representations, Complementary-Consensus Fusion (CCF) first repairs unreliable local evidence through complementary exchange and then enforces a shared spatial focus through consensus refinement. Extensive experiments on five MER benchmarks show that SAC$^2$-Net achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive performance across coarse-grained, fine-grained, large-scale, and cross-dataset evaluation settings.

AI.tldr: SAC$^2$-Net is a multimodal micro-expression recognition network that uses semantic anchors (AU-based text prompts) to align optical flow and motion magnification features, followed by reliability-aware fusion, achieving SOTA results on five benchmarks.
AI.motivation: MER is challenging due to subtle movements and limited data. Optical flow and motion magnification show asymmetric failure patterns (one modality fails while the other retains useful AU information), revealing complementarity but raising issues of cross-modal heterogeneity and spatially varying reliability.
AI.method: Proposes SAC$^2$-Net with two modules: (1) Semantic Anchoring Soft Alignment (SASA) converts activated AUs into textual prompts as semantic anchors to align motion-magnified and optical-flow features using hierarchical AU-aware soft labels; (2) Complementary-Consensus Fusion (CCF) repairs unreliable local evidence via complementary exchange and enforces shared spatial focus via consensus refinement.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive performance across coarse-grained, fine-grained, large-scale, and cross-dataset evaluation settings on five MER benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The asymmetric failure patterns of optical flow and motion magnification can be effectively leveraged through semantic-anchored alignment and reliability-aware fusion to improve MER performance.

title: TensorLDM: A Component-Wise Latent Diffusion Model for Volumetric DTI Reconstruction from Sparse DWIs
summary: Reconstructing diffusion tensors from sparse DWIs is critical for accelerating Diffusion Tensor Imaging (DTI) in clinical settings, yet current deep learning approaches frequently yield anatomically inconsistent or physically implausible tensors. We introduce TensorLDM, a component-wise latent diffusion model that processes the six tensor components through two group-specific encoders (for diagonal and off-diagonal elements) while maintaining anatomical consistency via shared DWI conditioning. TensorLDM uses an Anatomy-Conditioned Autoencoder that encourages the latent to focus on tensor properties rather than re-encoding structural information. A shared Cross-Component Attention (CCA) mechanism, applied in both autoencoder refinement and diffusion fine-tuning, models inter-component dependencies, while a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) DWI conditioner provides component-adaptive conditioning. On the Human Connectome Project (HCP) dataset under a single-shell, four-volume sparse acquisition, TensorLDM produces the most accurate downstream tractography and tensors with near-ground-truth physical validity (SPD-violation rate 1.54% vs. 1.40%), with the best or comparable voxel-wise reconstruction accuracy. Geodesic tensor error measured by the Log-Euclidean Metric (LEM) corroborates these gains.

AI.tldr: TensorLDM is a latent diffusion model for sparse DTI reconstruction that processes tensor components via group-specific encoders, uses anatomy-conditioned autoencoders, cross-component attention, and mixture-of-experts conditioning, achieving near-ground-truth physical validity on HCP data.
AI.motivation: Reconstructing diffusion tensors from sparse DWIs is critical for accelerating DTI in clinical settings, but current deep learning approaches frequently yield anatomically inconsistent or physically implausible tensors.
AI.method: TensorLDM uses: (1) two group-specific encoders for diagonal and off-diagonal tensor components, (2) an Anatomy-Conditioned Autoencoder to focus on tensor properties, (3) Cross-Component Attention (CCA) for inter-component dependencies, and (4) Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) DWI conditioner for component-adaptive conditioning.
AI.result: On HCP single-shell 4-volume sparse acquisition, TensorLDM achieves SPD-violation rate 1.54% (near ground-truth 1.40%), best tractography accuracy, and best/comparable voxel-wise reconstruction accuracy, with LEM corroborating gains.
AI.conclusion: TensorLDM effectively addresses anatomical and physical consistency issues in sparse DTI reconstruction, producing physically plausible tensors and accurate downstream tractography.

title: Disease-Centric Vision-Language Pretraining with Hybrid Visual Encoding for 3D Computed Tomography
summary: Vision-language pre-training (VLP) holds great promise for general-purpose medical AI by leveraging radiology reports as rich textual supervision, yet existing methods struggle with 3D CT imaging due to inefficient visual backbones and coarse semantic alignment. To address these issues, we propose a tailored VLP framework featuring three key components: (1) a CNN-ViT hybrid encoder that replaces ViT's patch embedding with a 3D CNN backbone to efficiently capture local anatomical details while preserving global attention and compatibility with pre-trained cross-modal priors; (2) a disease-level contrastive learning mechanism using learnable query tokens to dynamically extract disease-specific semantics from full reports and align them with corresponding visual features, thereby disentangling distinct diseases within the same anatomical region; and (3) a diagnosis-aware prompt strategy that employs real clinical phrases and aggregated disease prototypes to bridge the pre-training-inference gap and enhance zero-shot diagnostic reliability. Our model achieves state-of-the-art performance on CT-RATE (84.4% AUC, +5.1%) and Rad-ChestCT (75.4% AUC, +5.4%), with even larger gains (+9.8% AUC) on a challenging 60-disease benchmark, and demonstrates strong transferability to radiology report generation, underscoring the generality and clinical utility of our approach.

AI.tldr: A novel VLP framework for 3D CT imaging using a CNN-ViT hybrid encoder, disease-level contrastive learning, and diagnosis-aware prompt strategy achieves SOTA on multiple benchmarks and demonstrates strong transferability to report generation.
AI.motivation: Existing VLP methods struggle with 3D CT imaging due to inefficient visual backbones and coarse semantic alignment, limiting their clinical utility.
AI.method: (1) CNN-ViT hybrid encoder replacing ViT's patch embedding with 3D CNN; (2) disease-level contrastive learning with learnable query tokens for disease-specific alignment; (3) diagnosis-aware prompt strategy using real clinical phrases and disease prototypes.
AI.result: SOTA on CT-RATE (84.4% AUC, +5.1%), Rad-ChestCT (75.4% AUC, +5.4%), and +9.8% AUC on 60-disease benchmark; strong transferability to report generation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach demonstrates generality and clinical utility, significantly outperforming prior methods and supporting zero-shot diagnostics.

title: Efficient Cross-Scale Invertible Hiding Network with Spatial-Frequency Collaboration and Non-Invertible Mechanism
comment: IEEE TNNLS submitted by Junxue Yang, Xin Liao (https://msf-hnu.github.io/)
summary: Image hiding aims to conceal image-level messages within cover images at the same resolution. Invertible neural networks (INN)-based image hiding has emerged as an important branch. It treats concealing and revealing as a pair of inverse problems on image domain transformation and uses INN's forward and backward processes to address them. Due to architectural constraints, existing INN-based methods suffer from single-scale and single-domain feature extraction and limited nonlinear representation capability, resulting in inferior image quality. To mitigate these limitations, we propose an efficient cross-scale invertible hiding network with the spatial-frequency collaboration and the non-invertible mechanism, termed CrosInv. CrosInv exploits cross-scale and spatial-frequency collaborative features while enhancing nonlinear representation. Specifically, we introduce a cross-scale invertible module that bijectively maps inputs to cross-scale representations. To effectively integrate spatial and frequency information, the cross-scale invertible module employs pixel shuffle, Haar wavelet transformation, and their inverse operations for scale transformation. Furthermore, a non-invertible cross dense module is integrated to enhance the nonlinearity. Comprehensive experiments verify the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed CrosInv.

AI.tldr: CrosInv is a cross-scale invertible image hiding network with spatial-frequency collaboration and non-invertible mechanisms that significantly improves image quality over existing INN-based methods.
AI.motivation: Existing INN-based image hiding methods suffer from single-scale, single-domain feature extraction, and limited nonlinear representation capability, leading to inferior image quality.
AI.method: Proposes CrosInv with: (1) a cross-scale invertible module using pixel shuffle and Haar wavelet transforms for scale transformation, (2) spatial-frequency collaboration, and (3) a non-invertible cross dense module to enhance nonlinearity.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments verify the effectiveness and superiority of CrosInv over existing methods.
AI.conclusion: Cross-scale and spatial-frequency collaborative features combined with non-invertible mechanisms significantly improve image hiding quality.

title: Concept Removal for Frontier Image Generative Models
summary: Image generative models are trained on massive, largely uncurated internet-scale datasets that contain undesirable visual concepts. Efficiently removing such concepts from the model generations without degrading the quality of output images remains challenging. We introduce a novel concept removal method for frontier diffusion and image autoregressive models, such as SD3.5, Flux, and Infinity. Our intervention replaces the internal bottleneck layer present in all these modern models with a transcoder that is trained to replicate the original layer while structuring it into distinct activation features. This in-place substitution creates an integrated filter through which concept-specific signals can be selectively disabled while preserving the rest of the model's behavior. Since the intervention modifies the model backbone rather than attaching an external component, it remains persistent under white-box access. Empirically, the approach achieves state-of-the-art concept removal performance across modern diffusion and autoregressive models, maintains visual generation quality, provides robustness against adversarial prompts, and supports sequential removal of diverse concepts. This positions our method as a practical approach for concept removal in frontier image generative models.

AI.tldr: A method that replaces the internal bottleneck layer of modern image generative models (SD3.5, Flux, Infinity) with a transcoder to selectively disable concept-specific signals, achieving state-of-the-art concept removal while preserving generation quality and robustness against adversarial prompts.
AI.motivation: Internet-scale training data contains undesirable visual concepts, and existing removal methods degrade output quality or are easily bypassed.
AI.method: Replace the internal bottleneck layer of diffusion/autoregressive models with a transcoder that structures activations into distinct features, allowing selective disabling of concept-specific signals via in-place substitution.
AI.result: State-of-the-art concept removal performance across modern diffusion and autoregressive models, maintained visual quality, robustness against adversarial prompts, and support for sequential removal of multiple concepts.
AI.conclusion: The method provides a practical, persistent approach for concept removal in frontier image generative models under white-box access.

title: H-Adapter: Pose-Robust Hairstyle Transfer via Attention-Derived, Source-Aligned Hair Masks
summary: Hairstyle transfer has practical applications such as virtual try-on, yet remains challenging when the source and reference exhibit large head-pose discrepancies. We propose H-Adapter, which improves pose robustness by training with a region-specific loss that disentangles hair and non-hair objectives and thereby induces spatially disentangled cross-attention, from which a source-aligned hair edit mask is derived to guide diffusion-based inpainting. Experiments on pose-agnostic and pose-different subsets demonstrate strong quantitative results, including the best FID, $\mathrm{FID}_{\mathrm{CLIP}}$, and CLIP-I under pose differences, while maintaining competitive non-hair preservation and improving qualitative fidelity to fine-grained reference hairstyle details. Beyond source-conditioned transfer, H-Adapter supports practical extensions including text-to-image generation, auxiliary prompt-based hair color control, and compatibility with an identity-preserving IP-Adapter variant. We also introduce a VLM-as-a-judge protocol and observe consistent gains in hairstyle faithfulness, non-hair preservation, and artifact quality.

AI.tldr: H-Adapter is a pose-robust hairstyle transfer method using region-specific loss, disentangled cross-attention, and diffusion-based inpainting, achieving SOTA results on pose-different benchmarks while supporting text-to-image, hair color control, and identity preservation extensions.
AI.motivation: Hairstyle transfer is useful for virtual try-on but remains challenging when source and reference images have large head-pose discrepancies.
AI.method: Proposes H-Adapter with (1) region-specific loss for disentangling hair/non-hair objectives, (2) spatially disentangled cross-attention, (3) source-aligned hair edit mask for diffusion-based inpainting.
AI.result: Achieves best FID, FID_CLIP, and CLIP-I under pose differences; maintains competitive non-hair preservation; shows consistent gains via VLM-as-a-judge protocol.
AI.conclusion: H-Adapter improves pose robustness in hairstyle transfer, supports practical extensions (text-to-image, color control, identity preservation), and introduces a VLM-based evaluation protocol.

title: FeVOS: Foresight Expression Video Object Segmentation
summary: Existing Referring Video Object Segmentation tasks focus on referring expressions describing events, actions or appearances of relevant objects within the observed frames, lacking evaluation in scenarios that require pre-decisive spatio-temporal reasoning, thereby limiting their applicability. To address this, we propose Foresight Expression Video Object Segmentation, a task that queries future events in upcoming video segments and requires masks of the objects in the observed frames as visual answers. For example, in ego-centric scenes, the question "What tool will be used?" demands reasoning over spatio-temporal cues to predict the masks of the next tool to be used, which helps with the understanding of future actions and decisions. To support this task, we introduce FeVOS, a dataset with 968 video clips, 14,525 foresight expressions, and 2,904 chain-of-thought annotations to provide explicit and interpretable reasoning steps. We further develop FeVOS-R1, an MLLM-based model trained on our dataset via a two-stage pipeline of supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning. FeVOS-R1 not only achieves state-of-the-art performance on FeVOS, but also demonstrates strong generalization to existing RVOS benchmarks. We hope this work can inspire more research on predictive reasoning in video perception.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Foresight Expression Video Object Segmentation (FeVOS), a new task requiring models to predict future events by segmenting objects in current frames. The authors create a dataset (FeVOS) with 14K expressions and chain-of-thought annotations, and propose FeVOS-R1, an MLLM trained with supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning, achieving SOTA results.
AI.motivation: Existing RVOS tasks lack evaluation in scenarios requiring pre-decisive spatio-temporal reasoning about future events, limiting applicability in predictive settings like ego-centric action understanding.
AI.method: Introduced FeVOS dataset (968 clips, 14,525 foresight expressions, 2,904 CoT annotations) and FeVOS-R1, an MLLM trained via two-stage pipeline of supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning.
AI.result: FeVOS-R1 achieves state-of-the-art performance on FeVOS and demonstrates strong generalization to existing RVOS benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: This work inspires more research on predictive reasoning in video perception by bridging language-guided segmentation with future event prediction.

title: VPA-Guard: Defending and Benchmarking Image-to-Video Generation Against Visual Prompt Attacks
summary: Recent advancements in Image-to-Video (I2V) generation have transformed input images from simple appearance references into interactive control interfaces where visual cues such as arrows, sketches, and emojis orchestrate complex video dynamics with unprecedented controllability. However, these seemingly innocuous static cues can be interpreted by models as executable temporal instructions, unfolding into harmful actions in the generated videos. Despite the severity of this threat, existing safety benchmarks remain predominantly focused on text-based and content-only image-based jailbreaks, leaving implicit visual prompt attacks insufficiently explored. To bridge this gap, we present VVA-Bench, the first systematic benchmark for evaluating video generation safety under categorized vision-centric prompt attacks.
