Extensive experiments on VVA-Bench demonstrate that state-of-the-art models are highly susceptible to such attacks, with Attack Success Rates (ASR) reaching 100.0\% on Wan 2.7 and 74.8\% on Veo 3.1. To mitigate these risks, we propose VPA-Guard, a retrieval-augmented and self-evolving defense framework. By leveraging few-shot reasoning to identify latent malicious intents, our method reduces the attack ASR by 44.2\% and the harmfulness score by 73.4\% on average, while maintaining the model's utility for legitimate user edits. Our work provides both a rigorous benchmark and an effective defense strategy to advance safe and socially responsible multimodal generation.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces VVA-Bench, the first systematic benchmark for evaluating video generation safety under vision-centric prompt attacks, and proposes VPA-Guard, a retrieval-augmented defense framework that reduces attack success rates by 44.2% and harmfulness scores by 73.4% while preserving model utility.
AI.motivation: Existing safety benchmarks focus on text-based and content-only image-based jailbreaks, leaving implicit visual prompt attacks (e.g., arrows, sketches, emojis used as temporal instructions) insufficiently explored, despite these static cues being able to trigger harmful actions in generated videos.
AI.method: VVA-Bench systematically categorizes vision-centric prompt attacks for video generation safety evaluation. VPA-Guard is a retrieval-augmented and self-evolving defense framework that uses few-shot reasoning to identify latent malicious intents.
AI.result: Attack Success Rates reached 100.0% on Wan 2.7 and 74.8% on Veo 3.1 under vision-centric attacks. VPA-Guard reduces ASR by 44.2% and harmfulness score by 73.4% on average while maintaining utility for legitimate edits.
AI.conclusion: The work provides both a rigorous benchmark (VVA-Bench) and an effective defense strategy (VPA-Guard) to advance safe and socially responsible multimodal video generation.

title: Expresso-AI: Explainable Video-Based Deep Learning Models for Depression Diagnosis
comment: 8 pages. Accepted at the 2023 11th International Conference on Affective Computing and Intelligent Interaction (ACII). Code: https://github.com/felmoreno1726/Expresso-AI
summary: Given the widespread prevalence of depression and its consequential impact on individuals and society, it is crucial to obtain objective measures for early diagnosis and intervention. As a multidisciplinary topic, these objective measures should be interpretable and accessible to health care professionals, ensuring effective collaboration and treatment planning in the realm of mental health care. Even though current automated depression diagnosis approaches improved over the last decade, a critical gap exists as they often lack affect-specificity and interpretability, limiting their practical application and potential impact on mental health care. In particular, interpretability from temporal activities from videos when deep models are used is not fully explored. In this study, we present a novel framework for analyzing Deep Neural Networks' decisions when trained on facial videos, specifically focusing on automatic depression severity diagnosis. By fine-tuning Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNN) pre-trained on Action Recognition datasets on depression severity facial videos from AVEC depression dataset, our framework is able to interpret the model's saliency maps by examining face regions and temporal expression semantics. Our approach generates both visual and quantitative explanations for the model's decisions, providing greater insight into its reasoning. In addition to this interpretability, our video-based modeling has improved upon previous single-face benchmarks for visual depression diagnosis, resulting in enhanced predictive performance. Overall, our work demonstrates the successful development of a framework capable of generating hypotheses from a facial model's decisions while simultaneously improving depression's predictive capabilities.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an interpretable deep learning framework for automatic depression severity diagnosis from facial videos, using fine-tuned DCNNs with saliency maps to visualize and explain model decisions, while also improving predictive performance over prior benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Depression is widespread and impactful, yet current automated diagnosis methods lack affect-specificity and interpretability—especially regarding temporal facial activities in videos—limiting their clinical applicability and collaboration with healthcare professionals.
AI.method: The authors fine-tune Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNNs) pre-trained on Action Recognition datasets on depression severity facial videos from the AVEC dataset, and generate saliency maps to interpret model decisions by analyzing face regions and temporal expression semantics, producing both visual and quantitative explanations.
AI.result: The video-based modeling improved predictive performance compared to previous single-face benchmarks for visual depression diagnosis, while simultaneously providing interpretable saliency maps that offer insight into the model's reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully generates hypotheses from a facial model's decisions and enhances depression predictive capabilities, bridging the gap between deep learning performance and clinical interpretability.

title: ScaleHP: Estimating Hand Pose in Metric Space
comment: 27 pages, 8 figures, 6 tables; includes supplementary material
summary: Accurate metric-space hand pose estimation (HPE) is essential for immersive human-computer interaction and robotics. However, most existing methods predict poses in a root-relative coordinate system and cannot estimate the hand in absolute metric scale. In this work, we observe that the intrinsic proportional relationships among human hand bones encode stable anthropometric priors that implicitly correlate with the overall metric size of the hand. Leveraging this insight, we present ScaleHP, an end-to-end one-stage hand pose estimation framework that bypasses fragile extrinsic depth modules to recover the hand in metric space. ScaleHP employs a transformer-based decoder with a novel scale token to fuse multi-scale morphological and appearance features. By solving for metric coordinates through a perspective-constrained least-squares approach, we achieve high-precision pose estimation in the camera coordinate system. ScaleHP delivers state-of-the-art performance, including 35.8 CS-MPJPE on FreiHand and 4.6/5.9 PA-MPJPE on DexYCB and HO3Dv3. These results demonstrate that internal biological constraints significantly reduce relative geometry and absolute metric errors, offering a robust solution for generalized, real-world hand tracking.

AI.tldr: ScaleHP is a one-stage, end-to-end hand pose estimation framework that uses anthropometric bone proportion priors and a transformer-based scale token to directly recover metric-scale hand poses in camera coordinates, achieving state-of-the-art results on FreiHand, DexYCB, and HO3Dv3 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing hand pose estimation methods predict poses in a root-relative coordinate system and cannot estimate the hand in absolute metric scale, limiting real-world applications like human-computer interaction and robotics.
AI.method: ScaleHP uses (1) a transformer-based decoder with a novel scale token to fuse multi-scale morphological and appearance features, and (2) a perspective-constrained least-squares approach to recover metric coordinates in camera space, leveraging intrinsic proportional relationships among hand bones as stable anthropometric priors.
AI.result: Achieves 35.8 CS-MPJPE on FreiHand, 4.6/5.9 PA-MPJPE on DexYCB and HO3Dv3, setting state-of-the-art performance on all three benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Internal biological constraints from hand bone proportions significantly reduce both relative geometry and absolute metric errors, providing a robust solution for generalized, real-world hand tracking without relying on fragile extrinsic depth modules.

title: SSMNBench: Diagnosing Image-based Cross-View Human-Object Understanding via Single-View Sufficiency and Multi-View Necessity
comment: European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV). 32 pages, 10 figures. The code is available at: $ \href{https://github.com/gtc-gh/SSMNBench}{\text{SSMNBench}} $
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown remarkable progress in single-image perception, yet their ability to reason about complex cross-view human-centric scenes remains largely unverified. Current multi-view benchmarks evaluate models using a fixed "bag of frames" and thus conflate a model's robustness to visual distraction with its genuine ability to fuse fragmented cross-view evidence. To address this issue, we introduce SSMNBench, a diagnostic benchmark comprising 3,300 curated QA pairs for cross-view human and human-object understanding. SSMNBench uniquely categorizes tasks into Single-View Sufficiency (SVS) and Multi-View Necessity (MVN). By systematically perturbing view availability across 17 state-of-the-art MLLMs, critical limitations are revealed: models suffer from severe "distraction degradation" when presented with redundant views (SVS), and fail to integrate fragmented geometric evidence across cameras (MVN). Our evaluations demonstrate that modern MLLMs rely on multiple single-image semantic averaging and view preference rather than genuine cross-view synthesis. By exposing these fundamental vulnerabilities, SSMNBench provides a rigorous diagnostic framework to drive the advancement of future cross-view-aware multimodal architectures. The code is available at: $ \href{https://github.com/gtc-gh/SSMNBench}{\text{SSMNBench}} $

AI.tldr: SSMNBench is a diagnostic benchmark with 3,300 QA pairs that reveals current MLLMs fail at genuine cross-view reasoning—they suffer from distraction degradation on redundant views and cannot integrate fragmented evidence across cameras, relying instead on single-image semantic averaging and view preference.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-view benchmarks use a fixed "bag of frames" approach, which conflates a model's robustness to visual distraction with its actual ability to fuse fragmented cross-view evidence, making it unclear whether models truly reason across views.
AI.method: The authors introduce SSMNBench with 3,300 curated QA pairs for cross-view human and human-object understanding, categorizing tasks into Single-View Sufficiency (SVS) and Multi-View Necessity (MVN), and systematically perturbing view availability across 17 state-of-the-art MLLMs.
AI.result: Models suffer from severe "distraction degradation" on SVS tasks when given redundant views, and fail to integrate fragmented geometric evidence across cameras on MVN tasks. Modern MLLMs rely on single-image semantic averaging and view preference rather than genuine cross-view synthesis.
AI.conclusion: SSMNBench exposes fundamental vulnerabilities in current MLLMs' cross-view reasoning, providing a rigorous diagnostic framework to drive the advancement of future cross-view-aware multimodal architectures.

title: Auto-Labelling-Based Domain Transfer for 3D Object Detection on a Bicycle-Mounted LiDAR Platform
summary: Reliable 3D perception of vulnerable road users (VRUs) such as cyclists and pedestrians is essential for their safety in urban traffic and a core requirement for autonomous driving (AD). Alongside advances in vehicle-based perception, research increasingly equips bicycles with sensors to study traffic from a perspective native to VRUs. Such platforms still rely on LiDAR detectors originally trained on vehicle data, yet annotated 3D data from a cyclist's perspective is scarce. How well these detectors generalise to this setting has not been evaluated. We present a 3D object detection benchmark of 1,027 annotated LiDAR keyframes (over 18,000 3D bounding boxes) from the FUSE-Bike platform in urban Munich. We evaluate four nuScenes-pre-trained detectors against 1,854 human-verified ground-truth (GT) boxes both in their original form and after finetuning on training labels produced by a VRU-dedicated auto-labelling pipeline that requires no manual annotation. The zero-shot domain gap is concentrated on the VRU classes. Finetuning recovers most of it, improving mean average precision (mAP) by up to 23.4 points with the largest gains on pedestrians and cyclists, and the adapted detectors even surpass the quality of the auto-labels they were trained on. The benchmark provides a reproducible baseline for VRU-centric 3D detection and shows that auto-labels are a viable substitute for manual annotation when adapting vehicle-trained detectors to a cyclist platform.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a 3D object detection benchmark from a cyclist's perspective, showing that auto-labelling can effectively replace manual annotation to adapt vehicle-trained LiDAR detectors to VRU-centric scenarios.
AI.motivation: Annotated 3D data from a cyclist's perspective is scarce, yet VRU perception is critical for safety. The generalisation of vehicle-trained LiDAR detectors to this setting has not been evaluated.
AI.method: 1,027 annotated LiDAR keyframes (18,000+ 3D boxes) from the FUSE-Bike platform in Munich. Four nuScenes-pre-trained detectors evaluated against 1,854 human-verified GT boxes, with finetuning using auto-labelling pipeline requiring no manual annotation.
AI.result: Zero-shot domain gap concentrated on VRU classes; finetuning improved mAP by up to 23.4 points, with adapted detectors surpassing the quality of the auto-labels they were trained on.
AI.conclusion: Auto-labels are a viable substitute for manual annotation when adapting vehicle-trained detectors to a cyclist platform, providing a reproducible baseline for VRU-centric 3D detection.

title: Steering Vision-Language Models with Joint Sparse Autoencoders
summary: Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) have shown promise for analyzing language models, but applying them to vision-language models (VLMs) often yields representations that are difficult to use as controllable cross-modal steering directions. We introduce the Joint Sparse Autoencoder (JSAE), which uses an explicit alignment constraint to jointly factorize sequence-pooled vision and language activations into shared, interpretable image/caption-level features. Applied to LLaVA, JSAE recovers cross-modal features for recognizable concepts (e.g., food and animals). Through bidirectional interventions (additive steering and suppression), we observe a layer-dependent asymmetry under our protocol: additive steering peaks at mid-to-late (pre-output) layers and weakens at both ends, whereas suppression scores remain within a comparable range across all probed layers within statistical noise. Experiments on three VLMs, namely LLaVA-v1.6-Mistral-7B, Llama3-LLaVA-8B, and the MoE-based Qwen3-VL-30B, show related layer-localized effects across architectures. Together, these results suggest that explicitly aligned sparse representations support more controllable intervention-based analysis of multimodal features, within an identifiable layer range, than the unconstrained alternatives tested here.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Joint Sparse Autoencoder (JSAE) for aligning vision-language model representations into interpretable cross-modal features, revealing layer-dependent asymmetry in bidirectional intervention effectiveness.
AI.motivation: Standard SAEs applied to VLMs yield representations that are difficult to use as controllable cross-modal steering directions, motivating the need for explicitly aligned sparse representations.
AI.method: JSAE jointly factorizes sequence-pooled vision and language activations using an explicit alignment constraint into shared, interpretable features; tested via additive steering and suppression interventions across layers.
AI.result: Additive steering peaks at mid-to-late layers and weakens at both ends; suppression scores remain stable across layers. Effects are consistent across LLaVA-v1.6-Mistral-7B, Llama3-LLaVA-8B, and Qwen3-VL-30B.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly aligned sparse representations enable more controllable intervention-based analysis of multimodal features within an identifiable layer range, outperforming unconstrained alternatives.

title: Towards a Dynamic and Fixed-budget Memory Bank for Efficient Streaming Video Understanding
summary: Currently, streaming video understanding is still a daunting task for existing \emph{multimodal large language models} (MLLMs). Its difficulties not only lie in handling the ever-increasing video frames, but also in the unpredictability of future video content and input instructions. In this paper, we study this task from the perspective of constructing a dynamic but fixed-budget memory bank, and propose a novel and training-free approach termed \emph{\textbf{CausalMem}}. CausalMem is dedicated to constructing a dynamic visual memory update mechanism, thereby maximizing the amount of information in streaming video within a limited memory space, much like the human brain. In practice, CausalMem estimates the redundancy of visual tokens and updates the memory bank via an online semantic basis, which models the principal semantics of the observed video stream. To validate CausalMem, we apply it to two representative MLLMs, namely LLaVA-OneVision and Qwen2.5-VL respectively, and conduct extensive experiments on both streaming and offline video understanding benchmarks. The experimental results not only show the great advantages than existing methods under both streaming and offline settings, \emph{e.g.}, $+3.2\%$ and $+3.0\%$ average accuracy gains respectively, but also witness the superior semantic preservation for streaming videos, \emph{e.g.}, using 12$k$ token budgets to memorize hour-long streaming videos, which achieves more than \textbf{20$\times$} visual token compression ratio and only occupies about \textbf{82 MB} storage. \textbf{Our code} is given in \href{https://github.com/hktk07/CausalMem}{CausalMem}.

AI.tldr: CausalMem is a training-free, dynamic visual memory update mechanism for streaming video understanding in MLLMs. It uses an online semantic basis to estimate token redundancy and update a fixed-budget memory bank, achieving over 20x compression and superior performance on both streaming and offline benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Streaming video understanding remains challenging for MLLMs due to ever-increasing video frames and unpredictability of future content and instructions. Existing methods struggle to manage limited memory effectively while preserving essential video information over long streams.
AI.method: CausalMem constructs a dynamic fixed-budget memory bank with an online semantic basis that estimates visual token redundancy and updates memory accordingly. It is a training-free approach that mimics human memory consolidation by maximizing information within limited space.
AI.result: +3.2% accuracy gain on streaming benchmarks, +3.0% on offline benchmarks. Achieves 20x visual token compression ratio, storing hour-long streaming videos in 82 MB (12k token budget).
AI.conclusion: CausalMem effectively addresses streaming video understanding by providing a training-free, memory-efficient solution that outperforms existing methods in both streaming and offline settings, with strong semantic preservation capabilities.

title: Falcon: Functional Assembly and Language for Compositional Reasoning in X-ray
summary: Conventional vision-language models are largely object-centric, focusing on detecting and describing individual entities. In safety-critical X-ray baggage screening, however, threat often emerges not from a single object but from the functional compatibility of spatially dispersed components, such as batteries, detonators, and explosive charges. We formalize this setting as \emph{compositional threat reasoning}, where risk is modeled as a relational property of grounded regions rather than an independent detection outcome. We introduce \textbf{Falcon}, a multimodal framework that abstracts segmentation-aware region features into a structured safety state capturing component presence, pairwise functional compatibility, and scene-level risk. This structured representation is injected into the language model as an explicit intermediate interface, encouraging relationally consistent and safety-aware reasoning. To evaluate this problem, we present \textbf{Falcon-X}, a benchmark that unifies dense grounding with structured supervision over component completeness and risk inference in cluttered X-ray imagery. Experiments show that while existing multimodal models adapt to appearance, they struggle with compositional safety reasoning. Falcon improves functional grounding and produces more coherent threat assessments, establishing compositional safety reasoning as a distinct evaluation paradigm for multimodal systems.

AI.tldr: Falcon is a multimodal framework for X-ray baggage screening that models threat as relational properties between components rather than individual object detection, using structured safety state representations to improve compositional threat reasoning.
AI.motivation: Conventional vision-language models are object-centric and fail to capture threats arising from functional compatibility of spatially dispersed components (e.g., batteries + detonators + explosives) in safety-critical X-ray screening.
AI.method: Falcon extracts segmentation-aware region features, abstracts them into a structured safety state (component presence, pairwise functional compatibility, scene-level risk), and injects this representation into language models as an explicit intermediate interface for relationally consistent reasoning.
AI.result: Existing multimodal models adapt to appearance but struggle with compositional safety reasoning. Falcon improves functional grounding and produces more coherent threat assessments.
AI.conclusion: Compositional safety reasoning should be established as a distinct evaluation paradigm for multimodal systems.

title: What Does the Brain See? Multiview Neural Representations to Demystify the Brain-Visual Alignment
summary: Zero-shot visual decoding from electroencephalography (EEG) aims to infer visual semantics from non-invasive neural recordings, but remains challenging due to the low signal-to-noise ratio, non-stationarity, and limited spatial resolution of EEG. Existing EEG-vision alignment methods often rely on holistic EEG embeddings, which can obscure the complementary temporal, spectral, and spatial structure underlying visual perception. We introduce a unified multiview EEG representation learning framework for aligning brain responses with visual semantic embeddings. Our method builds an EEG encoder that jointly models three complementary views: input-conditioned state-space temporal dynamics, learnable wavelet-based spectral decomposition for sample-adaptive frequency modeling, and attention-modulated graph learning for structured electrode interactions. The resulting multiview EEG embeddings are fused and aligned with pretrained visual representations in a shared semantic space using contrastive learning with EEG-specific regularization, enabling 200-way zero-shot visual classification. Experiments on THINGS-EEG benchmark show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance, with 54.8% Top-1 and 85.6% Top-5 accuracy in the within-subject setting and 15.3% Top-1 and 45.4% Top-5 accuracy in the cross-subject setting. We further present the first systematic cross-session EEG-image decoding evaluation, achieving 40.8% Top-1 and 78.0% Top-5 accuracy. These results suggest that explicitly modeling multiview neural structure improves both semantic alignment and generalization in EEG-based visual decoding.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a multiview EEG representation learning framework that jointly models temporal, spectral, and spatial dynamics to align brain signals with visual semantics, achieving state-of-the-art zero-shot visual decoding on THINGS-EEG benchmark.
AI.motivation: Existing EEG-vision alignment methods rely on holistic EEG embeddings that fail to capture complementary temporal, spectral, and spatial structure underlying visual perception, limiting decoding performance.
AI.method: A unified multiview EEG encoder jointly modeling: (1) input-conditioned state-space temporal dynamics, (2) learnable wavelet-based spectral decomposition for adaptive frequency modeling, and (3) attention-modulated graph learning for structured electrode interactions. These views are fused and aligned with visual embeddings via contrastive learning with EEG-specific regularization.
AI.result: Achieves 54.8% Top-1 & 85.6% Top-5 (within-subject), 15.3% Top-1 & 45.4% Top-5 (cross-subject) on THINGS-EEG. First cross-session evaluation yields 40.8% Top-1 & 78.0% Top-5 accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling multiview neural structure (temporal, spectral, spatial) significantly improves semantic alignment and generalization in EEG-based zero-shot visual decoding.

title: Efficient Real-World Dehazing via Physics-Inspired Global-Local Decoupling
summary: Real-world single image dehazing is highly ill-posed due to spatially and spectrally varying scattering, while practical deployment demands lightweight and low-latency models. Existing approaches either rely on fragile physical inversion under simplified assumptions or adopt heavy blind architectures unsuitable for edge deployment. To overcome these limitations, we propose PGL-Net (Physics-Inspired Global-Local Decoupling Network), a lightweight framework that incorporates physical inductive biases via operator-level emulation, avoiding explicit parameter estimation. It decouples dehazing into global distribution rectification and local structural refinement. A Physics-Inspired Affine Fusion (PAF) module performs globally conditioned alignment across hierarchical skip connections to compensate for haze-induced bias, while a compact Degradation-Aware Modulation (DAM) block adaptively restores spatially and spectrally variant details through dynamic feature modulation. Extensive experiments on multiple real-world benchmarks demonstrate that PGL-Net achieves state-of-the-art restoration quality with significantly reduced complexity. Compared with the recent SOTA SGDN, the Tiny variant (PGL-Net-T) improves PSNR by up to 2.6dB and consistently enhances downstream object detection accuracy, while achieving over a 10x reduction in inference latency. Code is publicly available at: https://github.com/sc-30-bit/PGL-Net.

AI.tldr: PGL-Net is a lightweight, physics-inspired single image dehazing network that decouples the task into global rectification and local refinement using Physics-Inspired Affine Fusion (PAF) and Degradation-Aware Modulation (DAM). It achieves SOTA restoration quality with 10x+ lower latency than recent methods like SGDN.
AI.motivation: Real-world dehazing is ill-posed due to spatially/spectrally varying scattering, and existing methods either use fragile physical models under simplified assumptions or heavy architectures unsuitable for edge deployment.
AI.method: PGL-Net decouples dehazing into (1) global distribution rectification and (2) local structural refinement. It uses Physics-Inspired Affine Fusion (PAF) for globally conditioned skip-connection alignment, and a Degradation-Aware Modulation (DAM) block for spatially/spectrally variant detail restoration via dynamic feature modulation, avoiding explicit physical parameter estimation.
AI.result: PGL-Net-T (Tiny) improves PSNR by up to 2.6dB over SGDN, consistently boosts downstream object detection, and achieves over 10x reduction in inference latency.
AI.conclusion: PGL-Net demonstrates that incorporating physical inductive biases via operator-level emulation enables state-of-the-art dehazing quality with significantly reduced complexity, suitable for edge deployment.

title: UniTeD: Unified Temporal Diffusion for Joint Perception and Planning in Autonomous Driving
summary: Diffusion models have shown strong potential for multi-modal planning in end-to-end autonomous driving. However, most existing methods confine diffusion to the planning module, conditioning on fixed outputs from separate discriminative perception networks. This decoupled design propagates perception errors to the planner, increasing optimization difficulty and reducing robustness. To overcome these limitations, we propose UniTeD, a Unified Temporal Diffusion framework that jointly models perception and planning through iterative denoising in a shared generative space. By enabling bidirectional information exchange, the framework facilitates mutual refinement between tasks and improves robustness via noise-conditioned multi-task training. We further extend this unified diffusion paradigm to a streaming setting by incorporating temporal context. A Temporal Transition Module (TTM) is introduced to resolve the noise-level mismatch between historical and current frames. In addition, we propose an Anchor Refresh Strategy (ARS) to alleviate the training-inference distribution shift commonly observed in sparse diffusion-based end-to-end driving frameworks. Without bells and whistles, UniTeD achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks, surpassing both recent discriminative end-to-end methods and diffusion-based planning approaches.

AI.tldr: UniTeD proposes a unified temporal diffusion framework for end-to-end autonomous driving that jointly models perception and planning through iterative denoising, achieving state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion-based planning methods are decoupled from perception, relying on fixed outputs from separate discriminative networks, which propagates perception errors to the planner and reduces robustness.
AI.method: 1) Unified Temporal Diffusion framework (UniTeD) for joint perception and planning via shared iterative denoising. 2) Temporal Transition Module (TTM) for noise-level mismatch in streaming settings. 3) Anchor Refresh Strategy (ARS) to address training-inference distribution shift.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks, surpassing both recent discriminative end-to-end methods and diffusion-based planning approaches.
AI.conclusion: UniTeD demonstrates that unified generative modeling of perception and planning with bidirectional information exchange improves robustness and performance in end-to-end autonomous driving.

title: Point Cloud Diffusion with Global and Local Reconstruction for Instance-Level 3D Anomaly Detection
summary: 3D anomaly detection in point clouds is critical for high-precision industrial manufacturing. Reconstruction-based methods have laid a strong foundation by detecting 3D anomalies through comparisons between defective inputs and their reconstructed normal counterparts. However, existing methods still suffer from two challenges: 1) the foreground weak defective regions such as scratches are hard to reconstruct and detect, where the anomaly deviations in normalized point clouds can be as small as $10^{-3}$; 2) the background non-defective regions are prone to get positional bias in reconstruction, which leads to false positives. To address these challenges, we propose \textbf{PCDiff}, a point cloud diffusion framework for instance-level 3D anomaly generation and detection. In the generation phase, an instance-level multi-modal attention is embedded into the generation framework, where anomalies are conditioned with texture gradient, image patch, text and mask. The instance-level condition enables the high-quality generation of weak-defective anomalies. In the detection phase, a joint local-global reconstruction algorithm is introduced to ensure local anomaly restoration and global geometric consistency, which preserves background normal structure while restoring the foreground defect. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed PCDiff significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both 3D anomaly generation fidelity and reconstruction quality, leading to substantial improvements in anomaly detection accuracy.

AI.tldr: PCDiff is a point cloud diffusion framework for 3D anomaly generation and detection. It uses instance-level multi-modal attention for generating weak-defective anomalies and a joint local-global reconstruction algorithm to reduce false positives, outperforming existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing reconstruction-based 3D anomaly detection methods struggle with (1) foreground weak defects (e.g., scratches) that are hard to reconstruct due to tiny deviations (~10⁻³), and (2) background non-defective regions prone to positional bias causing false positives.
AI.method: Two-phase framework: (1) Generation phase — a diffusion model with instance-level multi-modal attention conditioning on texture gradient, image patch, text, and mask for high-quality weak-defect generation. (2) Detection phase — joint local-global reconstruction algorithm balancing local anomaly restoration and global geometric consistency.
AI.result: PCDiff significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in 3D anomaly generation fidelity and reconstruction quality, leading to substantial improvements in anomaly detection accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The proposed instance-level conditioning and joint local-global reconstruction effectively address the weak-defect detection and false positive problems, setting a new state-of-the-art for 3D point cloud anomaly detection.

title: Dual Distribution Estimation for Zero-shot Noisy Test-Time Adaptation with VLMs
summary: While test-time adaptation (TTA) empowers vision-language models to adapt without costly retraining, it remains highly vulnerable to out-of-distribution (OOD) outliers prevalent in real-world applications. This discrepancy motivates Noisy TTA (NTTA), an online task to filter noisy OOD samples on the fly while maximizing in-distribution (ID) classification accuracy. Existing zero-shot NTTA approaches typically rely on test-time discriminative training, leading to overconfident misclassifications and significantly degraded inference efficiency. To address these limitations, we propose a novel framework named Dual Distribution Estimation (DDE), shifting the zero-shot NTTA paradigm from instance-level learning to training-free Gaussian distribution modeling. DDE incorporates two novel modules: Positive Feature Distribution Estimation (PFDE) and Negative Label Distribution Estimation (NLDE). PFDE explicitly models class-wise inclusion and exclusion Gaussian distributions to formulate a calibrated contrastive score, robustly enhancing ID accuracy. In parallel, NLDE improves OOD identification by explicitly modeling the negative label distribution to mine highly discriminative labels, effectively mitigating spurious correlations. Extensive experiments show that on the large-scale ImageNet benchmark, DDE achieves an improvement of 3.70\% in harmonic mean accuracy and reduces the FPR95 for OOD detection by 6.20\%, while ensuring highly scalable and efficient online inference. Furthermore, DDE is zero-shot and training-free, demonstrating remarkable robustness in data-scarce scenarios. Codes are available at https://github.com/ZhuWenjie98/DDE.

AI.tldr: DDE is a zero-shot, training-free framework for Noisy Test-Time Adaptation that models Gaussian distributions for both positive features and negative labels, improving ID accuracy and OOD detection without costly training.
AI.motivation: Test-time adaptation (TTA) for vision-language models is highly vulnerable to OOD outliers. Existing zero-shot NTTA methods rely on test-time discriminative training, leading to overconfident misclassifications and degraded efficiency.
AI.method: DDE shifts from instance-level learning to training-free Gaussian distribution modeling, using two modules: PFDE (class-wise inclusion/exclusion Gaussian distributions for calibrated contrastive scores) and NLDE (negative label distribution modeling to mine discriminative labels).
AI.result: On ImageNet, DDE achieves 3.70% improvement in harmonic mean accuracy and reduces FPR95 for OOD detection by 6.20%, with highly scalable and efficient online inference.
AI.conclusion: DDE is zero-shot, training-free, robust in data-scarce scenarios, and effectively addresses the limitations of existing NTTA approaches.

title: ShutterMuse: Capture-Time Photography Guidance with MLLMs
summary: Real-world photography requires capture-time guidance for both camera framing and subject pose. Yet existing aesthetic cropping benchmarks mainly evaluate post-hoc crop prediction and overlook subject-side recommendations, leaving the capture-time guidance capabilities of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) underexplored. To address this gap, we introduce CaptureGuide-Bench, a benchmark with two complementary tasks: photographer-side composition decision and refinement, and subject-side scene-conditioned pose recommendation. Our evaluation reveals limitations: general-purpose MLLMs can make composition decisions but lack precise refinement localization, while specialized aesthetic cropping models localize crops effectively but are limited to refinement; neither provides actionable pose guidance. To support model development, we further construct CaptureGuide-Dataset, comprising 130K samples with textual rationales and structured visual annotations, and develop ShutterMuse, a unified MLLM trained with supervised and reinforcement fine-tuning. Experiments on CaptureGuide-Bench show that ShutterMuse achieves the best overall photographer-side performance among evaluated baselines and competitive subject-side pose recommendation with substantially lower inference cost, demonstrating the potential of MLLMs as interactive assistants for photography during image capture.

AI.tldr: CaptureGuide-Bench is a new benchmark evaluating MLLMs on two photography tasks: photographer-side composition and subject-side pose recommendation. Existing models have complementary weaknesses, so the authors create CaptureGuide-Dataset (130K samples) and ShutterMuse, a unified MLLM achieving strong performance with lower inference cost.
AI.motivation: Existing aesthetic cropping benchmarks evaluate post-hoc crop prediction but overlook subject-side pose recommendations and capture-time guidance capabilities of MLLMs, creating a gap this work aims to fill.
AI.method: The authors introduce CaptureGuide-Bench with two tasks (photographer-side composition & refinement, subject-side pose recommendation), construct CaptureGuide-Dataset (130K samples with rationales and annotations), and develop ShutterMuse (unified MLLM trained with supervised and reinforcement fine-tuning).
AI.result: General-purpose MLLMs make composition decisions but lack precise refinement; specialized cropping models localize crops well but only do refinement; neither provides pose guidance. ShutterMuse achieves best overall photographer-side performance and competitive subject-side pose recommendation with lower inference cost.
AI.conclusion: MLLMs show potential as interactive photography assistants during image capture, with ShutterMuse demonstrating unified capability across both composition and pose recommendation tasks efficiently.

title: $S^{2}$-FracMix: Label-Preserving Self-Saliency Mixup Augmentation
summary: Data augmentation is known to improve generalization of deep visual models. Recent methods favor mixup strategies that generate interpolated samples to improve model performance. However, these techniques not only incur significant computational overhead, they also lead to semantic disruption of augmentation data due to cross-sample mixing. We first propose Self-Saliency ($S^2$) Mixup, which constructs challenging yet label-consistent samples by extracting multi-scale salient patches and reinserting them into non-salient regions of the same image. This promotes scale-invariant feature learning while avoiding cross-sample interference. To further enhance model robustness, we introduce FracMix, a mixing scheme that injects self-similarity patterns into salient regions using adaptive ratios. Collectively, our unified framework, $S^{2}$-FracMix, enables simultaneous learning from fractal and non-fractal structures within a single image, yielding a targeted and structurally coherent augmentation strategy. We theoretically analyze the advantage of our technique, and empirically establish its superiority over the existing methods by achieving state-of-the-art performance in extensive evaluation with seven benchmarks across classification (coarse and fine-grained), robustness, calibration, object detection, and transfer learning tasks. Project page is available at \href{https://fracmix-data-augmentation.github.io/}{fracmix-data-augmentation.github.io}

AI.tldr: The paper proposes S²-FracMix, a unified data augmentation framework combining Self-Saliency Mixup (using multi-scale salient patches from the same image) and FracMix (injecting self-similarity patterns into salient regions), achieving state-of-the-art performance across classification, robustness, calibration, object detection, and transfer learning tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing mixup augmentation methods suffer from high computational overhead and semantic disruption caused by cross-sample mixing, motivating the need for a more efficient and label-consistent augmentation strategy.
AI.method: S²-FracMix combines Self-Saliency (S²) Mixup, which extracts multi-scale salient patches and reinserts them into non-salient regions of the same image, and FracMix, which injects self-similarity patterns into salient regions using adaptive ratios, enabling simultaneous learning from fractal and non-fractal structures within a single image.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across seven benchmarks spanning coarse/fine-grained classification, robustness, calibration, object detection, and transfer learning, with theoretical analysis supporting its advantages.
AI.conclusion: The proposed S²-FracMix framework provides a targeted and structurally coherent augmentation strategy that improves generalization while avoiding cross-sample semantic interference, outperforming existing augmentation methods.

title: Shift Variant Image Degradation and Restoration Using Singular Value Decomposition
summary: Shift-variant image degradation is frequently encountered in practical imaging systems where the point spread function (PSF) varies across the image field due to motion, optical aberrations, atmospheric turbulence, or sensor-related effects. Unlike shift-invariant, shift-variant degradation presents significant challenges for image restoration because the degradation process cannot be represented by a single convolution kernel. This paper proposes a singular value decomposition (SVD)-based framework for restoring images degraded by shift-variant motion blur. The proposed approach determines the contribution of small singular values using a singular-value energy retention criterion. Specifically, the number of small singular values is selected based on a specified percentage of cumulative singular-value energy, providing a systematic approach for controlling noise amplification while preserving useful image information. The degradation model is formulated using a position-dependent PSF represented by a shift-variant imaging operator. Three representative one dimensional shift-variant motion PSFs are considered: bidirectional linear motion, Gaussian motion, and simple harmonic motion. The image degradation process is modeled as a linear system, and SVD is employed to analyze and invert the corresponding degradation operator. The singular-value representation provides insight into the ill-conditioned nature of the restoration problem and enables the development of stable inversion techniques. The proposed SVD-based restoration algorithm is applied to three degraded images. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach in recovering image details and reducing blur artifacts under different motion models.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an SVD-based framework for restoring images degraded by shift-variant motion blur, using a singular-value energy retention criterion to control noise amplification while preserving image details.
AI.motivation: Shift-variant image degradation is common in real imaging systems (motion, aberrations, turbulence), but unlike shift-invariant degradation, it cannot be modeled by a single convolution kernel, making restoration significantly more challenging.
AI.method: The approach uses SVD to analyze the shift-variant degradation operator (position-dependent PSF). A singular-value energy retention criterion determines how many small singular values to keep, balancing noise suppression and information preservation. Three 1D motion models (bidirectional linear, Gaussian, simple harmonic) are tested.
AI.result: Experiments on three degraded images demonstrate the proposed method effectively recovers image details and reduces blur artifacts under different shift-variant motion models.
AI.conclusion: The SVD-based framework with energy retention criterion provides a systematic and stable approach to shift-variant image restoration, addressing the ill-conditioned nature of the problem.

title: Edges Before Embeddings: A Confidence-Aware Blur Gate for Vision-Language Pipelines
summary: Production vision pipelines silently degrade on blurry input, wasting compute on downstream OCR, retrieval, and vision-language model (VLM) calls that cannot recover a usable output. We present MagikaDocumentFromPixel, a lightweight, CPU-friendly image quality gate that classifies a single image as sharp, blurred, or uncertain in roughly 7 ms on a single CPU core. The contributions are (i) a recipe selected from a 46-configuration, 8-sweep empirical search that isolates input resolution as the dominant lever and shows architecture capacity only pays off at >= 384 px; (ii) a confidence-aware routing formalism grounded in classical selective prediction; (iii) the Edge Prior Module (EPM), a Laplacian-magnitude auxiliary input channel that gives the network direct access to the spectral evidence that classical blur heuristics rely on and that lifts test F1 by +1.3 points in a matched-env comparison; and (iv) an observation that the gate is one instance of a recurring design pattern that appears independently in Magika content-type detection, risk-controlled OCR with VLMs, and DocVLM. The final recipe MobileNetV3-Large with the EPM trained at 384x384 on paired GoPro Large frames, evaluated with 5-scale test-time augmentation reaches F1 = 0.9803 (AUC 0.9989) with a 17 MB ONNX artifact, improving over our fixed-scale baseline on the same hardware (F1 = 0.9672) by +1.31 points. We are explicit about limitations: results are on a single motion-blur distribution, numbers are from a single seed, and calibration is qualitative rather than measured.

AI.tldr: MagikaDocumentFromPixel is a lightweight CPU-based image blur gate (7ms per image) that uses MobileNetV3-Large with an Edge Prior Module (Laplacian auxiliary channel) trained at 384×384, achieving F1=0.9803 (AUC 0.9989) with a 17MB ONNX model, designed to prevent wasted compute in downstream vision pipelines.
AI.motivation: Production vision pipelines waste compute on blurry inputs that cause downstream OCR, retrieval, and VLM calls to fail. There is a need for a fast, lightweight quality gate to filter blurry images before expensive processing.
AI.method: The authors conducted a 46-configuration, 8-sweep empirical search to identify optimal settings (isolating input resolution as key lever), introduced a confidence-aware routing formalism based on selective prediction, designed the Edge Prior Module (EPM) using Laplacian magnitude as an auxiliary input channel, and trained MobileNetV3-Large at 384×384 on paired GoPro Large frames with 5-scale test-time augmentation.
AI.result: F1=0.9803 (AUC 0.9989), 7ms per image on a single CPU core, 17MB ONNX artifact. The EPM lifted test F1 by +1.3 points compared to a matched-environment baseline (0.9672 → 0.9803).
AI.conclusion: The proposed gate effectively and efficiently filters blurry inputs in vision pipelines. The authors note limitations: single motion-blur distribution, single-seed results, and qualitative (not measured) calibration.

title: Graph it first! Enabling Reasoning on Long-form Egocentric Videos through Scene Graphs
summary: Existing multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) face significant challenges in processing long video sequences due to strict input token limitations. As a result, current video understanding approaches, especially in egocentric settings characterized by complex dynamics, frequent state changes, and moving cameras, are forced to massively subsample frames. This leads to severe loss of temporal and contextual information, constraining their ability to perform fine-grained video reasoning. In this work, we introduce a framework for egocentric video question answering (VQA) that overcomes these input constraints through Egocentric Scene Graphs (EgoSGs), i.e., temporally grounded, structured representations that capture objects, attributes, spatial relations, and interactions over time. By representing videos as compact, text-based scene graphs, our method preserves the essential visual and temporal information of the original video in a symbolic form that drastically reduces input length while maintaining semantic richness. Crucially, this enables MLLMs to reason efficiently over entire video sequences within their token budget. On HD-EPIC VQA, our method achieves state-of-the-art results, outperforming strong video-based baselines on multiple models and suggesting that structured, temporally grounded representations like EgoSGs can bridge long-form egocentric video understanding and the context limitations of today's MLLMs.

AI.tldr: A framework using Egocentric Scene Graphs (EgoSGs) to compress long egocentric videos into compact, text-based structured representations, enabling MLLMs to perform video QA within token limits and achieving SOTA on HD-EPIC VQA.
AI.motivation: Existing MLLMs have strict input token limits, forcing heavy frame subsampling in long video understanding, which loses temporal/contextual information—especially problematic for egocentric videos with complex dynamics and frequent state changes.
AI.method: Proposes Egocentric Scene Graphs (EgoSGs)—temporally grounded, structured representations capturing objects, attributes, spatial relations, and interactions over time, converting videos into compact text-based graph formats that preserve semantic richness while drastically reducing token length.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on HD-EPIC VQA, outperforming strong video-based baselines across multiple models.
AI.conclusion: Structured, temporally grounded representations like EgoSGs effectively bridge the gap between long-form egocentric video understanding and the context limitations of current MLLMs.

title: Naturalness Predicts but Does Not Cause Transferability in Image Encodings of Real-World Streams
comment: 9 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables; code and data manifest included as ancillary files
summary: A common practice converts a one-dimensional signal into an image so that a vision backbone pretrained on natural photographs can be reused for recognition, yet the encoded image is rarely examined. We ask how the visual naturalness of an encoded image relates to its transfer accuracy under a frozen backbone. We build WorldStream, a corpus of 299 heterogeneous current-value series from key-free public APIs (weather, air quality, earthquakes, gold and oil, equities, crypto, foreign exchange, web activity and space weather), with a nine-way source-recognition task over 3143 temporally split windows. Across seven encodings and six frozen backbones, the Frechet distance of an encoding to natural images (FID) predicts its accuracy: Spearman $ρ=-0.72$. Two controlled interventions show this is not causal in the spectrum. Our invertible encoder has a single adjustable part, a spectral exponent $β$ (power $\propto |f|^{-β}$); varying $β$ moves the image toward or away from the natural-image manifold at fixed content. FID is lowest near the natural value $β\approx 2$, but frozen accuracy stays flat and far below the structured baselines (19.2% vs. 73.0%), and FID and accuracy are only weakly related over the sweep (Pearson $-0.32$). A second intervention, phase scrambling, holds the power spectrum exactly fixed while removing local structure; now FID and accuracy fall together (Pearson $-0.89$). The cross-encoding correlation is thus mediated by local structure, not spectral naturalness: FID predicts accuracy because Inception reads the same structure the backbones do. Full fine-tuning does not close the gap (27% vs. 67%), so the deficit is structural. The encoder is exactly invertible, recovering the signal from the 8-bit image at 72.9 dB, so the image doubles as a lossless record of the data.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates why converting 1D signals to images for vision backbone reuse works, finding that FID predicts accuracy (ρ=-0.72) due to shared local structure perception, not spectral naturalness, and that even fine-tuning cannot close the gap (27% vs 67%), while their invertible encoder achieves 72.9 dB reconstruction.
AI.motivation: To understand how the visual naturalness of encoded 1D signals relates to transfer accuracy when using frozen vision backbones pretrained on natural images, and to disentangle whether spectral similarity or local structural features drive the predictive power of FID.
AI.method: Built WorldStream corpus (299 time series from 9 sources, 3143 windows), tested 7 encodings × 6 frozen backbones, used FID as naturalness metric, and conducted two controlled interventions: (1) varying spectral exponent β (power law) while fixing content, and (2) phase scrambling while fixing power spectrum.
AI.result: FID predicts accuracy across encodings (Spearman ρ=-0.72). Varying β alone shows FID lowest near β≈2 but accuracy flat (~19.2% vs 73.0% baseline), weak relationship (Pearson -0.32). Phase scrambling causes FID and accuracy to fall together (Pearson -0.89). Fine-tuning only reaches 27% vs 67%.
AI.conclusion: The FID-accuracy correlation is mediated by shared local structure perception, not spectral naturalness. The encoder is exactly invertible (72.9 dB recovery), enabling lossless data storage via images.

title: USS: Unified Spatial-Semantic Prompts for Embodied Visual Tracking with Latent Dynamics Learning
summary: Embodied Visual Tracking (EVT) requires an agent to continuously follow a specified target while actively moving through dynamic environments. However, prevailing EVT paradigms predominantly rely on language-based target indication. While language is expressive and convenient, cluttered scenes often contain multiple objects that satisfy the same semantic description, leading to ambiguous target grounding. We therefore propose a paradigm shift, reframing target indication in EVT from text-only specification to unified spatial-semantic prompting. Based on this paradigm, we introduce Unified Spatial-Semantic Prompts for Embodied Visual Tracking with Latent Dynamics Learning, USS, an end-to-end embodied tracking framework that supports text, point, bounding box, and mask prompts within a unified architecture. USS encodes heterogeneous prompts with modality-specific encoders, fuses prompt tokens with visual features through hybrid attention, and decodes compact prompt-conditioned representations into egocentric waypoints. To further improve temporal robustness, USS incorporates a latent world model that predicts future representations through self-supervised alignment. Real-robot experiments demonstrate that explicit spatial target cues yield higher success rates than text-only prompts, particularly in scenarios involving similar distractors and longer-horizon tracking where maintaining instance-level target identity is critical. In the simulation benchmark, USS also achieves state-of-the-art performance among non-MLLM-based methods and competitive results against recent MLLM-based approaches with faster inference speed. Our findings reveal that spatial-semantic prompting provides a more precise and flexible target indication interface for embodied visual tracking. Project site: https://arescheah.github.io/uss-project-page/.

AI.tldr: USS introduces a unified spatial-semantic prompting framework for embodied visual tracking, supporting text, point, bounding box, and mask prompts within one architecture, using a latent world model for temporal robustness, achieving higher success rates than text-only methods especially in cluttered scenes.
AI.motivation: Current EVT methods rely on text-only target descriptions, which cause ambiguous grounding in cluttered scenes with multiple objects matching the same semantic label. The authors propose shifting to unified spatial-semantic prompting for more precise target indication.
AI.method: USS is an end-to-end framework that encodes heterogeneous prompts (text, point, box, mask) via modality-specific encoders, fuses prompt tokens with visual features via hybrid attention, and decodes representations into egocentric waypoints. A latent world model predicts future representations via self-supervised alignment for temporal robustness.
AI.result: Real-robot experiments show spatial cues outperform text-only prompts in cluttered scenes and long-horizon tracking. On simulation benchmarks, USS achieves SOTA among non-MLLM methods and competitive results vs MLLM-based approaches with faster inference.
AI.conclusion: Spatial-semantic prompting offers a more precise and flexible target indication interface for embodied visual tracking, significantly improving instance-level target disambiguation.

title: Enhancing Brain MRI Anomaly Detection and Reasoning with ROI Rethink and Synthetic Data
summary: Medical vision-language models typically generate diagnoses through single-pass inference without indicating which image regions support their conclusions. This lack of spatial grounding limits clinical utility: outputs cannot be audited, and models may hallucinate findings on normal scans. We present BrReMark (Brain Rethink via ROI Marking), a framework that introduces explicit region marking into brain MRI diagnosis. The model first generates hypotheses about potential abnormalities and grounds them through explicit bounding box marking, then verifies conclusions by re-examining the marked evidence. Training combines supervised fine-tuning on structured reasoning trajectories with reinforcement learning using a composite reward over localization accuracy and diagnostic reasoning. Furthermore, we integrate a domain randomization-based pathology synthesis augmentation strategy to improve the model's generalizability to out-of-distribution (OOD) data. On internal benchmark, BrReMark improves mAP50 from 0.74% to 37.54% compared to the base model, while achieving 21.57% Clinical F1 and 45.26% diagnostic accuracy. On NOVA OOD benchmark, it also achieves competitive overall performance with a 45.7% reduction in false positives compared to the state-of-the-art, indicating reduced hallucination on rare pathologies. These findings suggest that explicit hypothesis-verification grounding is a practical path toward trustworthy open-ended brain MRI diagnosis across both in-distribution and OOD settings.

AI.tldr: BrReMark is a framework for brain MRI diagnosis that explicitly marks regions of interest, generates hypotheses, and verifies them through re-examination, improving localization accuracy and reducing hallucinations on both in-distribution and out-of-distribution data.
AI.motivation: Medical vision-language models generate diagnoses without spatial grounding, limiting clinical auditability and causing hallucination on normal scans. The authors aim to introduce explicit region marking for trustworthy, auditable diagnosis.
AI.method: BrReMark uses a hypothesis-verification pipeline: first generates abnormality hypotheses with bounding box markings, then re-examines marked evidence to verify conclusions. Training combines supervised fine-tuning on reasoning trajectories with reinforcement learning using composite rewards (localization + diagnostic accuracy). Pathology synthesis via domain randomization is used for OOD generalization.
AI.result: On internal benchmark, mAP50 improved from 0.74% to 37.54%, with 21.57% Clinical F1 and 45.26% diagnostic accuracy. On NOVA OOD benchmark, achieves competitive performance with 45.7% reduction in false positives vs. state-of-the-art.
AI.conclusion: Explicit hypothesis-verification grounding is a practical path toward trustworthy open-ended brain MRI diagnosis in both in-distribution and OOD settings.

title: SurgAtlas: A Large-Scale Surgical Video-Language Dataset with 2,391 Hours of Open and Minimally Invasive Surgery
summary: We introduce SurgAtlas, the largest surgical video-language dataset to date, comprising 15,291 videos (2,391 hours) spanning 18 surgical specialties and over 5,000 procedure types, sourced entirely from publicly available YouTube content. SurgAtlas is also the first surgical video-language dataset to include open surgery at scale, with 6,182 open procedure videos alongside over 9,000 minimally invasive recordings, and the first to establish standardized benchmarks for open-surgery video understanding. We additionally provide an expert-validated subset with verified visual question-answer pairs across diverse open and minimally invasive procedures, serving as a clinically grounded benchmark for surgical reasoning. Compared with existing surgical video-language datasets, SurgAtlas provides one of the most diverse annotation schemas, combining segment-level captions, step- and phase-level descriptions, video-level surgical descriptions, and reasoning-oriented question-answer pairs organized within a hierarchical taxonomy. These annotations are constructed through an automated multi-tier pipeline with LLM-based enrichment and a staged VQA generation framework with explicit groundedness verification. The scale and diversity of SurgAtlas enable training surgical foundation models with broad procedural coverage: we finetune Qwen3-VL-8B through a two-stage captioning-then-instruction pipeline and achieve competitive or state-of-the-art results on multiple established surgical benchmarks, including phase recognition, triplet detection, and reasoning question answering. More broadly, SurgAtlas provides a large native public video corpus that can support future large-scale pretraining of multimodal surgical AI systems and contribute to the development of next-generation foundation models for surgery.

AI.tldr: SurgAtlas is the largest surgical video-language dataset (15,291 videos, 2,391 hours, 18 specialties) with the first large-scale open surgery benchmark, expert-validated VQA pairs, diverse annotation schemas, and an automated multi-tier annotation pipeline. Fine-tuning Qwen3-VL-8B achieves SOTA on phase recognition, triplet detection, and reasoning QA.
AI.motivation: Existing surgical video-language datasets are limited in scale, diversity, and coverage of open surgery. There is a need for large-scale, publicly available data to train multimodal surgical foundation models with broad procedural understanding.
AI.method: Collected 15,291 YouTube surgical videos; built an automated multi-tier annotation pipeline with LLM-based enrichment and staged VQA generation with explicit groundedness verification; provided segment, step, phase, and video-level captions plus reasoning QA pairs; fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-8B via two-stage captioning-then-instruction pipeline.
AI.result: Achieved competitive or state-of-the-art results on phase recognition, triplet detection, and reasoning question answering across multiple established surgical benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: SurgAtlas provides a large native public video corpus for future large-scale pretraining of multimodal surgical AI systems and contributes to next-generation foundation models for surgery.

title: OracleAnalyser: Analysing Implicit Semantics of Oracle Bone Scripts through MLLMs with Post-training
summary: With the advancement of artificial intelligence, research on oracle bone scripts has entered a new era. However, existing methods and benchmarks remain largely confined to recognition tasks, overlooking the equally crucial aspect of oracle bone analysis. To address this gap, we propose OracleAnalyser, a reasoning framework for oracle bone analysis based on post-training techniques. Specifically, we fine-tune Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct through multiple post-training stages and introduce a new preference optimization algorithm, Stable Focal Preference Optimization (SFPO), tailored to the characteristics of oracle bone datasets. In addition, we release both an oracle bone reasoning dataset and an oracle bone preference dataset, and further construct a new benchmark to evaluate models' analytical capabilities for oracle bone scripts. Extensive experiments validate the superior analytical performance of OracleAnalyser, which achieves remarkable results with only 3B parameters, surpassing models with substantially larger scales.

AI.tldr: OracleAnalyser is a 3B-parameter reasoning framework for oracle bone script analysis, fine-tuned from Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct using a novel SFPO algorithm, achieving superior performance over larger models on a new benchmark.
AI.motivation: Existing research on oracle bone scripts focuses mainly on recognition tasks, while the crucial aspect of oracle bone analysis (reasoning-level understanding) is overlooked.
AI.method: Fine-tunes Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct through multiple post-training stages; introduces Stable Focal Preference Optimization (SFPO) for preference alignment; releases oracle bone reasoning and preference datasets.
AI.result: OracleAnalyser (3B parameters) surpasses substantially larger models on the newly constructed benchmark for oracle bone analytical capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Post-training techniques and preference optimization can effectively enhance VLMs for specialized historical document analysis, achieving strong performance at small model scale.

title: In-context Region-based Drag: Drag Any Region to Any Shape
comment: Accepted by ECCV 2026. Dataset, code, and model are available at https://github.com/bcmi/ICRDrag-Region-Drag-Editing
summary: Diffusion models have shown promise in drag-style editing. Previous works mainly focus on point-based drag, which is inherently ambiguous. This paper focuses on region-based drag and introduces a novel In-Context Region-based Drag (ICRDrag) method. Under the in-context learning framework, ICRDrag consumes a source image, a source region mask, and a target region mask, producing the target dragged image. Built upon the basic in-context learning model, we introduce two novel attention regularization: 1) image-mask attention consistency to ensure that a target region attends to similar source regions for image and mask modalities; 2) source-target attention correspondence to ensure the mutual correspondence between source and target regions. To facilitate region-based drag, we also construct Paired Region Dataset (PRD), a large-scale dataset with paired masks and images. Extensive experiments show that ICRDrag significantly outperforms existing methods in both quantitative metrics and user studies, achieving superior editing accuracy and visual fidelity. The dataset, code, and model are available at https://github.com/bcmi/ICRDrag-Region-Drag-Editing.

AI.tldr: ICRDrag is a region-based drag editing method using in-context learning with two attention regularizations, outperforming existing point-based drag approaches.
AI.motivation: Previous point-based drag editing is inherently ambiguous; this paper aims to enable more precise region-based drag editing in diffusion models.
AI.method: Proposes ICRDrag under an in-context learning framework with two novel attention regularizations: 1) image-mask attention consistency, 2) source-target attention correspondence. Also constructs a Paired Region Dataset (PRD) for training.
AI.result: Significantly outperforms existing methods in both quantitative metrics and user studies, achieving superior editing accuracy and visual fidelity.
AI.conclusion: Region-based drag editing with in-context learning and attention regularization is more effective than point-based approaches for diffusion model drag editing.

title: FunPiQ: A New Benchmark for Pixel-Level Quality Assessment in Fundus Images
comment: Accepted at MICCAI 2026 main conference. Our code, weights, and dataset are available at https://github.com/penway/FunPiQ
summary: Color fundus photography (CFP) is the most common ophthalmic imaging modality for large-scale screening. However, it is highly susceptible to degradations, making robust fundus image quality assessment (FIQA) crucial. The criteria for what constitutes high-quality at the image level vary across clinical tasks, making FIQA dependent on expert knowledge. This motivated the development of automated methods and datasets. While existing datasets aim to standardize image-level quality, their criteria often differ. Furthermore, image-level labels preclude the quantitative evaluation of localized degradations, which is essential for trustworthy FIQA. We argue that pixel-level FIQA based on anatomical visibility represents a more task-agnostic, explainable approach. In this work, we introduce FunPiQ, the first FIQA benchmark to provide pixel-level quality annotations. In addition, we propose EFIQA-CP, an explainable-by-design (EBD) method that uses quality pseudo-labels based on anatomical visibility to train a CNN via Non-Negative Positive-Unlabeled learning. Extensive evaluations of classification methods with post-hoc explanations, anomaly detection methods, and EBD methods demonstrate the superior performance of the last and, particularly, of EFIQA-CP.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces FunPiQ, the first pixel-level fundus image quality assessment benchmark, and proposes EFIQA-CP, an explainable-by-design method using anatomical visibility and Non-Negative Positive-Unlabeled learning for robust quality assessment.
AI.motivation: Color fundus photography is common but susceptible to degradations, and existing image-level quality assessment lacks the ability to evaluate localized degradations and is task-dependent. The authors argue for a pixel-level, task-agnostic, and explainable approach based on anatomical visibility.
AI.method: FunPiQ benchmark provides pixel-level quality annotations. EFIQA-CP uses quality pseudo-labels based on anatomical visibility and trains a CNN via Non-Negative Positive-Unlabeled (NNPU) learning, designed to be explainable-by-design (EBD).
AI.result: Extensive evaluations comparing classification methods with post-hoc explanations, anomaly detection methods, and EBD methods show that EBD methods, particularly EFIQA-CP, achieve superior performance.
AI.conclusion: Pixel-level FIQA based on anatomical visibility is a more task-agnostic and explainable approach, and the proposed EFIQA-CP method outperforms other approaches in fundus image quality assessment.

title: Pulmonary Embolism Risk Stratification from CTPA and Medical Records: Vascular Graphs Are Not All You Need
comment: 8 1/2 pages + 2 pages of references. Accepted for MICCAI 2026. This preprint has not undergone peer review or any post-submission improvements or corrections. The Version of Record of this contribution is published in, and available online at, the external reference provided below
summary: Risk stratification for pulmonary embolism (PE) is critical for clinical decision-making. Stratification guidelines are based on patient medical records, parameters measured from computed tomography pulmonary angiography (CTPA), and blood tests. However, blood tests are often missing in routine practice. This work studies whether state-of-the-art models can accurately classify risk stratification from only medical records and biomarkers extracted from CTPA images. We benchmark different approaches to combine medical records and cardiac biomarkers with rich pulmonary vascular information; we add vascular biomarkers to tabular models and apply graph neural networks (GNNs) on the vascular tree's intrinsic graph representation. We use a private dataset (n=353) with uniquely complete data for PE risk stratification. Our results show that, among global features, medical records and cardiac biomarkers are the most significant predictors, while vascular biomarkers do not further improve stratification. Even more surprising, even GNNs on vascular graphs fail to outperform strong tabular baseline on global features. We consider hypotheses, on both models and data, that could explain this suboptimal performance. Our investigation suggests that, counter-intuitively, vascular graphs might hold no discriminative information for PE risk stratification. Code is available from https://github.com/creatis-myriad/GENESIS.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether PE risk stratification can be accurately performed using only medical records and CTPA-derived biomarkers, finding that vascular biomarkers and graph neural networks on vascular trees do not improve upon tabular models using global features, suggesting vascular graphs may lack discriminative information for this task.
AI.motivation: Blood tests are often missing in routine practice, so the authors aim to determine if PE risk stratification can be accurately performed without them, using only medical records and CTPA image biomarkers.
AI.method: The authors benchmark tabular models with global features (medical records + cardiac biomarkers), add vascular biomarkers, and apply graph neural networks on vascular tree graph representations, using a private dataset (n=353) with complete PE risk stratification data.
AI.result: Medical records and cardiac biomarkers are the most significant predictors. Vascular biomarkers do not improve stratification, and GNNs on vascular graphs fail to outperform strong tabular baselines on global features.
AI.conclusion: Vascular graphs might hold no discriminative information for PE risk stratification, counter-intuitively, challenging the assumption that detailed vascular structure analysis adds value beyond global features.

title: A Benchmark for Heterogeneous Stereo Deblurring with Physically- and Epipolar-constrained Cross Attention
summary: Modern stereo-capable smartphones enable immersive XR content capture. However, hardware heterogeneity across camera modules often causes severe asymmetric blur artifacts. Existing methods and benchmarks largely assume homogeneous stereo setups and therefore do not explicitly address such asymmetric degradation. To bridge this gap, we present a dedicated framework for heterogeneous stereo deblurring. First, we introduce the heterogeneous stereo deblurring (HSD) dataset, constructed from real smartphone stereo captures via multi-frame integration. Second, we propose physically- and epipolar-constrained cross attention (PECA), a lightweight module that restricts cross-view matching to an epipolar search window bounded by a optics-derived disparity upper bound. By enforcing physically valid disparity constraints, PECA enables efficient and reliable cross-view feature fusion. Moreover, our confidence-weighted attention with residual fusion emphasizes cross-guided deblurring when correspondences are reliable, while naturally falling back to self-deblurring in occluded or unreliable regions. PECA is architecture-agnostic and consistently improves CNN-, Transformer-, and NAFNet-based baselines. Extensive experiments on HSD show that PECA-enhanced models achieve improved restoration performance with favorable efficiency.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a framework for heterogeneous stereo deblurring, including a new HSD dataset and a lightweight PECA module that uses physically- and epipolar-constrained cross attention for efficient cross-view feature fusion, improving diverse baseline architectures.
AI.motivation: Modern smartphones with stereo cameras suffer from asymmetric blur due to hardware heterogeneity, but existing methods assume homogeneous stereo setups and fail to address this degradation.
AI.method: 1) HSD dataset from real smartphone stereo captures via multi-frame integration. 2) Physically- and epipolar-constrained cross attention (PECA) module that restricts cross-view matching to an epipolar search window bounded by optics-derived disparity upper bound. 3) Confidence-weighted attention with residual fusion that emphasizes cross-guided deblurring when reliable and falls back to self-deblurring otherwise.
AI.result: PECA is architecture-agnostic and consistently improves CNN-, Transformer-, and NAFNet-based baselines. PECA-enhanced models achieve improved restoration performance with favorable efficiency on the HSD dataset.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses heterogeneous stereo deblurring through physically-constrained cross attention, enabling efficient and reliable asymmetric blur removal across diverse architectures.

title: Taxonomy-aware deep learning for hierarchical marine species classification in underwater imagery
comment: 10 pages, 3 figures, 4 tables. Presented at SPIE Defense + Security 2026 (Machine Learning from Challenging Data conference), National Harbor, MD, April 2026
summary: Automated classification of marine species from underwater imagery is essential for scalable ocean biodiversity monitoring and conservation policy. Existing approaches struggle with severe domain shift across collection platforms, fine-grained visual similarity between closely related species, and uneven annotation granularity, where many specimens can only be identified to genus or a coarser taxonomic rank. We present a taxonomy-aware deep learning framework that aligns both the training loss and the inference rule with the hierarchical structure of biological classification, combining a taxonomy-weighted loss, minimum-risk Bayesian inference, multi-scale feature encoding, and independent per-rank classification heads. Evaluated on the FathomNet 2025 dataset1 (79 marine classes across seven taxonomic ranks), the system achieves a mean taxonomic distance of 1.581, within 3% of the 1st-place solution (1.535), with the largest gains from metric-aligned inference and simple, decoupled components that generalize better than learned dependencies under distribution shift.

AI.tldr: A taxonomy-aware deep learning framework for marine species classification uses hierarchical loss, Bayesian inference, multi-scale features, and per-rank heads to achieve near-state-of-the-art results on the FathomNet 2025 benchmark (mean taxonomic distance 1.581, within 3% of 1st place), with decoupled components generalizing better than complex learned dependencies under domain shift.
AI.motivation: Automated marine species classification from underwater imagery is needed for scalable biodiversity monitoring, but existing methods struggle with domain shift across platforms, fine-grained visual similarity between species, and uneven annotation granularity (many specimens only identifiable to genus or coarser rank).
